The Lynching of Emmett Till.summary: Emmett Louis Till was an African-American boy who, at 14 years old, was abducted and lynched in Mississippi in 1955 after being accused of offending a white woman, Carolyn Bryant, in her family's grocery store. The brutality of his murder and the acquittal of his killers drew attention to the long history of violent persecution of African Americans in the United States. Till posthumously became an icon of the civil rights movement.

Montgomery Bus Boycott.summary: The Montgomery bus boycott was a political and social protest campaign against the policy of racial segregation on the public transit system of Montgomery, Alabama. It was a foundational event in the civil rights movement in the United States. The campaign lasted from December 5, 1955—the Monday after Rosa Parks, an African-American woman, was arrested for her refusal to surrender her seat to a white person—to December 20, 1956, when the federal ruling Browder v. Gayle took effect, and led to a United States Supreme Court decision that declared the Alabama and Montgomery laws that segregated buses were unconstitutional.

Integration of Little Rock.description: African-American students enrolled at a previously segregated high school
Integration of Little Rock.summary: The Little Rock Nine were a group of nine African American students enrolled in Little Rock Central High School in 1957. Their enrollment was followed by the Little Rock Crisis, in which the students were initially prevented from entering the racially segregated school by Orval Faubus, the Governor of Arkansas. They then attended after the intervention of President Dwight D. Eisenhower.

SNCC and Student Organizing.description: Activist organization during the civil rights movement
SNCC and Student Organizing.summary: The Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, and later, the Student National Coordinating Committee was the principal channel of student commitment in the United States to the civil rights movement during the 1960s. Emerging in 1960 from the student-led sit-ins at segregated lunch counters in Greensboro, North Carolina, and Nashville, Tennessee, the Committee sought to coordinate and assist direct-action challenges to the civic segregation and political exclusion of African Americans. From 1962, with the support of the Voter Education Project, SNCC committed to the registration and mobilization of black voters in the Deep South. Affiliates such as the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and the Lowndes County Freedom Organization in Alabama also worked to increase the pressure on federal and state government to enforce constitutional protections.

Freedom Riders.description: American civil rights activists of the 1960s
Freedom Riders.summary: Freedom Riders were civil rights activists who rode interstate buses into the segregated Southern United States in 1961 and subsequent years to challenge the non-enforcement of the United States Supreme Court decisions Morgan v. Virginia (1946) and Boynton v. Virginia (1960), which ruled that segregated public buses were unconstitutional. The Southern states had ignored the rulings and the federal government had done nothing to enforce them. The first Freedom Ride left Washington, D.C., on May 4, 1961, and was scheduled to arrive in New Orleans on May 17.

The Murder of Medgar Evers.summary: Medgar Wiley Evers was an American civil rights activist who was the NAACP's first field secretary in Mississippi. A United States Army veteran who served in World War II, he was engaged in efforts to overturn racial segregation at the University of Mississippi, end the segregation of public facilities, and expand opportunities for African Americans, including the enforcement of voting rights prior to his assassination.

aliases: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom
March on Washington.summary: The March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom was held in Washington, D.C., on August 28, 1963. The purpose of the march was to advocate for the civil and economic rights of African Americans. At the march, several popular singers of the time, including Mahalia Jackson and Marian Anderson, performed and many of the movement's leaders gave speeches. The most notable speech came from the final speaker, Martin Luther King Jr., standing in front of the Lincoln Memorial, as he delivered his historic "I Have a Dream" speech in which he called for an end to legalized racism and racial segregation.

Freedom Summer.description: 1964 voter registration campaign in Mississippi, U.S.
Freedom Summer.summary: Freedom Summer, also known as Mississippi Freedom Summer, was a campaign launched by American civil rights activists in June 1964 to register as many African-American voters as possible in the state of Mississippi.

Civil Rights Act of 1964.description: Landmark U.S. civil rights and labor law
Civil Rights Act of 1964.summary: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 is a landmark civil rights and labor law in the United States that outlaws discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, and national origin. It prohibits unequal application of voter registration requirements, racial segregation in schools and public accommodations, and employment discrimination. The act "remains one of the most significant legislative achievements in American history".

Assassination of Malcolm X.summary: Malcolm X was an African American revolutionary and human rights activist who founded Muslim Mosque, Inc. (MMI) and the Organization of Afro-American Unity (OAAU). He was also a prominent figure during the civil rights movement until his assassination in 1965. A spokesman and minister for the Nation of Islam (NOI) from 1952 until 1964, after which he left, he was a vocal advocate for Black empowerment and the promotion of Islam within the African American community. A controversial figure accused of preaching violence, Malcolm X is also a celebrated figure with Black people and Muslims worldwide for his pursuit of racial justice.

Selma to Montgomery.description: 1965 nonviolent protests for African-American voting rights in Alabama, United States
Selma to Montgomery.summary: The Selma to Montgomery marches were three protest marches, held in 1965, along the 54-mile (87 km) highway from Selma, Alabama, to the state capital of Montgomery. The marches were organized by nonviolent activists to demonstrate the desire of African-American citizens to exercise their constitutional right to vote, in defiance of segregationist repression; they were part of a broader voting rights movement underway in Selma and throughout the American South. By highlighting racial injustice, they contributed to passage that year of the Voting Rights Act, a landmark federal achievement of the civil rights movement.

Voting Rights Act.description: US federal legislation that prohibits racial discrimination in voting
Voting Rights Act.summary: The Voting Rights Act of 1965 is a landmark U.S. federal statute that prohibits racial discrimination in voting. It was signed into law by President Lyndon B. Johnson during the height of the civil rights movement on August 6, 1965. Congress later amended the Act five times to expand its protections. Designed to enforce voting rights protected by the Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments to the United States Constitution, the Act sought to secure the right to vote for racial minorities throughout the country, especially in the South. According to the U.S. Department of Justice, the Act is considered to be the most effective piece of federal civil rights legislation ever enacted. The National Archives and Records Administration stated: "The Voting Rights Act of 1965 was the most significant statutory change in the relationship between the federal and state governments in the area of voting since the Reconstruction period after the Civil War".

Black Panther Party.summary: The Black Panther Party was an American Marxist–Leninist and black power political and militant organization founded by college students Bobby Seale and Huey P. Newton in October 1966 in Oakland, California and active in that area until 1982. Between 1968 and 1971, it was also a nationwide organization with chapters in many major American cities, including San Francisco, New York City, Chicago, Los Angeles, Seattle, and Philadelphia. Members were active in many prisons and had international chapters in the United Kingdom and Algeria. The party first drew attention for openly carrying firearms in Oakland while monitoring police activity. Its earliest goal was to challenge the excessive force and misconduct of the Oakland Police Department that affected the African American community during the civil rights movement. It advocated for decent housing, community control of education and police, exemption from military service, and free breakfast for children. Party members were involved in many fatal firefights with police.

The Brown Berets.summary: The Brown Berets is a pro-Chicano paramilitary organization that emerged during the Chicano Movement in the United States during the late 1960s. David Sanchez and Carlos Montes co-founded the group modeled after the Black Panther Party. The Brown Berets was part of the Third World Liberation Front. It worked for educational reform, farmworkers' rights, and against police brutality and the Vietnam War. It also sought to separate the American Southwest from the control of the United States government.

The Long Hot Summer.description: Race riots in the US in 1967
The Long Hot Summer.summary: The long, hot summer of 1967 refers to a period of widespread racial unrest across major American cities during the summer of 1967, where over 150 riots erupted, primarily fueled by deep-seated frustrations regarding police brutality, poverty, and racial inequality within black communities. The term emphasizes the intensity and widespread nature of the urban violence that summer.

Assassination of MLK.wikipedia_title: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.
Assassination of MLK.summary: On April 4, 1968, at 6:01 p.m. CST, Martin Luther King Jr., an American civil rights activist, was fatally shot at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, Tennessee. He was rushed to St. Joseph's Hospital, where he was pronounced dead at 7:05 p.m. at age 39.

Civil Rights Act of 1968.summary: The Civil Rights Act of 1968 is a landmark law in the United States signed into law by President Lyndon B. Johnson during the King assassination riots.

Greensboro sit-ins.description: 1960 nonviolent protests at segregated lunch counters in Greensboro, North Carolina
Greensboro sit-ins.summary: The Greensboro sit-ins were a series of nonviolent protests in February to July 1960, primarily at the Woolworth's store in Greensboro, North Carolina, which led to the F. W. Woolworth Company department store chain removing its policy of racial segregation in the Southern United States. The sit-ins began on February 1, 1960, when four African American college students from North Carolina A&T State University -- Ezell Blair Jr. (later Jibreel Khazan), David Richmond, Franklin McCain, and Joseph McNeil -- sat down at the segregated lunch counter and were refused service. They returned the next day with more students. The protest movement spread to other Southern cities within weeks and helped catalyze the founding of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) in April 1960 at Shaw University in Raleigh.

Brown v. Board of Education.description: 1954 US Supreme Court decision declaring school segregation unconstitutional
Brown v. Board of Education.summary: Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka, 347 U.S. 483 (1954), was a landmark decision of the U.S. Supreme Court ruling that U.S. state laws establishing racial segregation in public schools are unconstitutional, even if the segregated schools are otherwise equal in quality. The decision was issued on May 17, 1954. It partially overruled the Court's 1896 decision Plessy v. Ferguson, which had held that racial segregation laws did not violate the U.S. Constitution as long as the facilities for each race were equal in quality, a doctrine that had come to be known as 'separate but equal.' The Brown decision was unanimous and was written by Chief Justice Earl Warren. The case was argued by Thurgood Marshall on behalf of the NAACP Legal Defense Fund. The decision is widely considered one of the most consequential of the 20th century and laid the legal foundation for the civil rights movement.

Southern Christian Leadership Conference.description: African-American civil rights organization founded in 1957
Southern Christian Leadership Conference.summary: The Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) is an African-American civil rights organization based in Atlanta, Georgia. It was founded on January 10, 1957, after the success of the Montgomery bus boycott, by Martin Luther King Jr., Ralph Abernathy, Fred Shuttlesworth, Joseph Lowery, Bayard Rustin, Ella Baker, and other Black ministers and activists. King served as its first president. SCLC was a major organization in the African-American civil rights movement and played a leading role in the 1961-62 Albany Movement, the 1963 Birmingham campaign, the 1963 March on Washington, the 1965 Selma to Montgomery marches, the 1966 Chicago Freedom Movement, and the 1968 Poor People's Campaign. After King's assassination in 1968, Ralph Abernathy succeeded him as president.

Congress of Racial Equality.description: American civil rights organization founded in 1942
Congress of Racial Equality.summary: The Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) is an African-American civil rights organization founded in Chicago in 1942 by an interracial group of students including James L. Farmer Jr., George Houser, James R. Robinson, Joe Guinn, Bernice Fisher, and Homer Jack. CORE was founded as a pacifist, interracial organization committed to nonviolent direct action drawn from Mahatma Gandhi's principles. CORE pioneered the use of nonviolent direct-action tactics, including sit-ins, jail-ins, and the 1961 Freedom Rides, which it co-organized with SNCC and SCLC. CORE played a major role in Freedom Summer in 1964; James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner -- the three Freedom Summer workers murdered in Mississippi in June 1964 -- were CORE staff. James Farmer served as CORE's national director from 1961 to 1966.

NAACP.description: American civil rights organization founded in 1909
aliases: National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
NAACP.summary: The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) is an American civil rights organization formed in 1909 as a biracial, biregional endeavor to advance justice for African Americans by a group including W. E. B. Du Bois, Mary White Ovington, Moorfield Storey, Ida B. Wells, and Lillian Wald. The NAACP's stated mission is to ensure the political, educational, social, and economic equality of rights of all persons and to eliminate race-based discrimination. The NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund, originally part of the NAACP but spun off as a separate organization in 1957 under Thurgood Marshall's leadership, was the principal legal vehicle of the strategy that led to Brown v. Board of Education in 1954. Medgar Evers served as the NAACP's first field secretary in Mississippi from 1954 until his assassination on June 12, 1963. Roy Wilkins served as Executive Director from 1955 to 1977 through the central decades of the civil rights movement.

Sixteenth Street Baptist Church bombing.description: 1963 white-supremacist bombing of a Black church in Birmingham, Alabama
Sixteenth Street Baptist Church bombing.summary: The 16th Street Baptist Church bombing was an act of white supremacist terrorism that occurred at the African-American 16th Street Baptist Church in Birmingham, Alabama, on Sunday, September 15, 1963. Four members of a local Ku Klux Klan chapter planted at least 15 sticks of dynamite attached to a timing device beneath the steps on the east side of the church. At about 10:22 a.m., as 26 children were walking into the basement assembly room to prepare for the sermon, the bomb exploded. Four girls were killed: Addie Mae Collins (age 14), Cynthia Wesley (age 14), Carole Robertson (age 14), and Carol Denise McNair (age 11). At least 14 others were injured. Although the FBI concluded in 1965 that the bombing had been committed by four known Ku Klux Klan members, no prosecutions occurred until 1977, when Robert Edward Chambliss was tried and convicted of first-degree murder. The case was reopened by the FBI in 1980, leading to the eventual convictions of Thomas Edwin Blanton Jr. in 2001 and Bobby Frank Cherry in 2002. The bombing was a pivotal moment in the civil rights movement and is widely credited with helping galvanize support for the Civil Rights Act of 1964.

Bloody Sunday.description: March 7, 1965 attack on civil rights marchers at the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma, Alabama
Bloody Sunday.summary: Bloody Sunday refers to the events of March 7, 1965, the first of the three Selma to Montgomery marches. Approximately 600 civil rights marchers, led by John Lewis of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) and Hosea Williams of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC), began the planned 54-mile march from Selma, Alabama, to the state capital in Montgomery to demand voting rights for African Americans, following the killing of Jimmie Lee Jackson by an Alabama state trooper in nearby Marion three weeks earlier. When the marchers reached the Edmund Pettus Bridge on the eastern edge of Selma, they were attacked by Alabama state troopers and Dallas County sheriff's deputies wielding billy clubs and tear gas. John Lewis suffered a fractured skull and at least 17 marchers were hospitalized. Television footage of the brutal attack, broadcast nationally that evening, shocked the country and produced a wave of support for the voting rights movement, contributing significantly to the passage of the Voting Rights Act of 1965 five months later.

Mississippi Burning.wikipedia_title: Murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner
Mississippi Burning.description: 1964 murders of three civil rights workers in Neshoba County, Mississippi
Mississippi Burning.summary: The murders of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner, also known as the Freedom Summer murders or the Mississippi Burning murders, occurred on June 21, 1964 in Neshoba County, Mississippi. The three civil rights workers -- James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner -- were members of the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) and its constituent organization the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE). They had been investigating the burning of Mt. Zion Methodist Church near Philadelphia, Mississippi, as part of the Freedom Summer voter registration campaign when they were arrested for an alleged traffic violation, briefly held in the Neshoba County jail, then released into the night and ambushed by members of the Mississippi White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan, including Cecil Price, the deputy sheriff of Neshoba County. The disappearance of the three young men, two of them white Northerners (Goodman and Schwerner), triggered a massive FBI investigation that the Bureau code-named 'Mississippi Burning' (MIBURN). Forty-four days later, their bodies were found buried in an earthen dam. Although federal charges were brought in 1967 and seven Klansmen were convicted of federal civil rights violations, no state murder charges were brought until 2005, when Edgar Ray Killen was convicted of three counts of manslaughter on the 41st anniversary of the murders.

Albany Movement.summary: The Albany Movement was a desegregation coalition formed in Albany, Georgia, on November 17, 1961, by local activists, the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), and the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP). Charles Sherrod and Cordell Reagon had arrived in Albany earlier in 1961 to organize on behalf of SNCC. Dr. William G. Anderson, a local osteopath, was named the movement's first president. In December 1961, Martin Luther King Jr. and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) joined the movement after King was arrested at a peaceful prayer vigil -- this was King's first time being jailed for civil disobedience. The Albany campaign was largely considered a tactical failure: Albany police chief Laurie Pritchett's strategy of nonviolent mass arrest dispersed across multiple jurisdictions denied the movement the dramatic confrontations that had built national support for Montgomery, and the city's segregation ordinances remained intact when King and other SCLC leaders left in August 1962. The Albany experience nonetheless gave the SCLC strategic lessons -- particularly about choosing campaign targets where local police were more likely to provide the visible brutality that would galvanize national attention -- lessons applied directly in the 1963 Birmingham campaign.

Orangeburg Massacre.description: February 8, 1968 killing of three Black students by South Carolina Highway Patrol officers at South Carolina State College
Orangeburg Massacre.summary: The Orangeburg Massacre occurred on the night of February 8, 1968, when South Carolina Highway Patrol officers opened fire on approximately 200 unarmed protesters on the campus of South Carolina State College (now South Carolina State University) in Orangeburg, South Carolina. The protesters, mostly Black students, were demonstrating against racial segregation at the All-Star Bowling Lanes, the only bowling alley in Orangeburg. Three young Black men were killed: Samuel Ephesians Hammond Jr., a freshman; Henry Ezekial Smith, a sophomore; and Delano Herman Middleton, a 17-year-old high school student. At least 27 other people were wounded. Among the wounded was Cleveland Sellers, a Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee organizer who was the only person later convicted in connection with the events, serving seven months in prison on a charge of incitement to riot; Sellers was pardoned in 1993 and later received an honorary doctorate from South Carolina State University. Nine highway patrolmen were tried in federal court in 1969 on charges of imposing summary punishment without due process and all were acquitted. The Orangeburg Massacre occurred more than two years before the better-known Kent State shootings of 1970 but received far less media coverage at the time.

Memphis sanitation strike.description: 1968 Black sanitation workers' strike in Memphis, Tennessee that drew Martin Luther King Jr. to the city
Memphis sanitation strike.summary: The Memphis sanitation strike began on February 12, 1968, when more than 1,300 Black sanitation workers walked off the job in Memphis, Tennessee, to protest unsafe working conditions, abusive treatment by white supervisors, and a stark wage gap between Black and white workers. The strike was triggered by the deaths of Echol Cole and Robert Walker, two sanitation workers who were crushed to death by a malfunctioning garbage compactor on February 1, 1968. The workers' iconic 'I AM A MAN' placards became enduring symbols of the civil rights movement and of the link between civil rights and labor rights. Martin Luther King Jr. came to Memphis multiple times to support the strike. On the evening of April 3, 1968, he delivered his 'I've Been to the Mountaintop' speech at the Mason Temple. The next day, April 4, 1968, King was assassinated at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis at 6:01 p.m. CST. The strike was settled twelve days after his assassination, on April 16, 1968, when the city of Memphis recognized the workers' union (American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees Local 1733) and granted modest wage increases.

Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party.description: 1964 multi-racial Mississippi political party that challenged the all-white state delegation at the Democratic National Convention
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party.summary: The Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP) was an American political party formed on April 26, 1964 as a multi-racial branch of the Mississippi Democratic Party. Its purpose was to challenge the legitimacy of the all-white delegation that Mississippi was sending to the 1964 Democratic National Convention, given that Black Mississippians had been systematically excluded from voter registration and political participation in the state. Key MFDP organizers included Aaron Henry, the Reverend Ed King, Fannie Lou Hamer, Bob Moses, Lawrence Guyot, and Annie Devine. At the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City, New Jersey in August 1964, MFDP delegates demanded to be seated as Mississippi's legitimate representatives. Fannie Lou Hamer's nationally televised testimony before the Convention's Credentials Committee, describing the brutal beating she received in a Mississippi jail for attempting to register to vote, became one of the most memorable moments of the civil rights era. President Lyndon B. Johnson, concerned about Southern white Democrats walking out, sought to block live coverage of Hamer's testimony and brokered a compromise offering the MFDP two non-voting at-large seats and a promise to bar segregated delegations from future conventions; the MFDP rejected the compromise as inadequate. The MFDP's challenge nonetheless transformed national party politics: the 1968 Democratic National Convention seated an integrated Mississippi delegation, and the modern Democratic Party's procedural rules requiring racially representative delegations trace directly to the MFDP's 1964 challenge.

Highlander Folk School.description: American training school for labor and civil rights organizers, founded 1932 in Tennessee
Highlander Folk School.summary: Highlander Folk School (later renamed the Highlander Research and Education Center) is an American training and education center founded in 1932 by Myles Horton, Don West, and James Dombrowski near Monteagle, Tennessee. Highlander played a key role in the American labor movement of the 1930s and 1940s and an even more central role in the civil rights movement of the 1950s and 1960s. The school trained civil rights leaders and grassroots organizers in workshops on nonviolent resistance, voter registration, citizenship, and community organizing. Rosa Parks attended a Highlander workshop in the summer of 1955, months before her December 1955 arrest in Montgomery sparked the bus boycott. Martin Luther King Jr., John Lewis, Diane Nash, James Bevel, Bernard Lafayette, and many other Movement leaders attended Highlander workshops. The folk song 'We Shall Overcome,' which became the unofficial anthem of the civil rights movement, was adapted at Highlander by music director Zilphia Horton and later popularized by Pete Seeger, who was a frequent visitor and teacher at the school. In 1961, the State of Tennessee revoked Highlander's charter and confiscated its property as part of a sustained campaign by segregationists to shut the school down; Highlander reorganized as the Highlander Research and Education Center and continued its mission first from Knoxville and then, from 1972 onward, from a campus near New Market, Tennessee.

Letter from Birmingham Jail.description: April 1963 open letter written by Martin Luther King Jr. from a Birmingham jail cell defending nonviolent resistance against racism
Letter from Birmingham Jail.summary: Letter from Birmingham Jail (also called 'Letter from a Birmingham Jail' and 'The Negro Is Your Brother') is an open letter written on April 16, 1963 by Martin Luther King Jr. King wrote it while imprisoned in the Birmingham city jail, where he had been confined on April 12 for participating in nonviolent demonstrations against segregation as part of the Birmingham campaign. The letter was a response to 'A Call for Unity,' a public statement made on April 12, 1963 by eight white Alabama clergymen who agreed that social injustices existed but argued that the battle against racial segregation should be fought solely in the courts, not in the streets, and described King's nonviolent demonstrations as 'unwise and untimely.' King's letter defended the strategy of nonviolent resistance against racism, arguing that people have a moral responsibility to break unjust laws and to take direct action rather than waiting potentially forever for justice to come through the courts. The letter became one of the most important written documents of the civil rights movement; it included King's famous line 'Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere.' First written on the margins of a newspaper smuggled into the jail, then on scraps of paper supplied by a friendly Black prisoner, the letter was later typed up by King's secretary Wyatt Tee Walker after King was released, and was first published in May 1963 in The Christian Century and Liberation.

Birmingham Children's Crusade.description: May 1963 march by thousands of Black children in Birmingham, Alabama, to protest segregation
Birmingham Children's Crusade.summary: The Children's Crusade, or Children's March, was a march by more than a thousand Black school students in Birmingham, Alabama, on May 2-3, 1963, as part of the broader Birmingham campaign organized by the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) under the direction of Martin Luther King Jr. and James Bevel. The march was conceived in response to the dwindling numbers of adults willing to be arrested as the campaign wore on; Bevel proposed using school-age children, reasoning that they had less to lose economically and would dramatize the moral stakes of segregation. On May 2, more than a thousand students walked out of their schools to attempt to march to downtown Birmingham; hundreds were arrested in waves. On May 3, Birmingham Public Safety Commissioner Eugene 'Bull' Connor ordered police and firefighters to use high-pressure fire hoses and police dogs against the marching children. The televised images of children being assaulted by water cannons and police dogs galvanized national and international opinion against the segregationist establishment, became one of the most iconic visual records of the civil rights movement, and contributed directly to the passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. The Birmingham campaign as a whole also resulted in an agreement to desegregate downtown stores and to release jailed protesters.

Watts riots.description: August 1965 large-scale uprising in the Watts neighborhood of Los Angeles, California
Watts riots.summary: The Watts riots, also known as the Watts uprising or the Watts rebellion, took place in the Watts neighborhood of Los Angeles, California, from August 11 to 16, 1965. The unrest began on August 11 with the traffic stop of Marquette Frye, a 21-year-old Black motorist suspected of drunk driving, by a California Highway Patrol officer near Avalon Boulevard. The encounter escalated into a physical confrontation involving Frye, his brother, his mother, and bystanders, and a crowd gathered and grew hostile to the police. Over the next six days, civil unrest spread across approximately 50 square miles of South Central Los Angeles. The California Army National Guard was called in, reaching nearly 14,000 troops on the scene by August 14. By the time the unrest ended on August 16, 34 people had been killed, more than 1,000 were injured, nearly 4,000 had been arrested, and property damage was estimated at $40 million. The riots followed years of accumulated grievances over police brutality, housing discrimination, unemployment, and segregated schools in the Watts neighborhood and the broader Black community of Los Angeles, and they marked an inflection point in public perception of the civil rights movement -- attention shifted from Southern segregation to systemic inequality in Northern and Western American cities.

Poor People's Campaign.description: 1968 SCLC effort organized by Martin Luther King Jr. to gain economic justice for poor people in the United States
Poor People's Campaign.summary: The Poor People's Campaign, or Poor People's March on Washington, was a 1968 effort organized by Martin Luther King Jr. and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) and carried out under the leadership of Ralph Abernathy following King's assassination on April 4, 1968. The campaign demanded economic and human rights for poor Americans of diverse backgrounds. King and the SCLC announced the campaign in December 1967 with the goal of building a multi-racial coalition of poor people to march on Washington and demand federal action to address poverty. After King's assassination at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis, where he had traveled to support the Memphis sanitation workers' strike, Abernathy and other SCLC leaders carried the campaign forward. From May 12 to June 24, 1968, thousands of protesters set up an encampment called Resurrection City on the National Mall in Washington, D.C., adjacent to the Lincoln Memorial. The campaign included demands for jobs, unemployment insurance, a fair minimum wage, and education funding for poor children. The encampment was forcibly closed by the Park Police on June 24, 1968. The Poor People's Campaign produced modest legislative gains but is generally remembered as the final major SCLC campaign of the King era and as a turning point that broadened the civil rights movement's focus from racial segregation to systemic economic inequality.

Loving v. Virginia.description: 1967 US Supreme Court decision striking down laws banning interracial marriage
Loving v. Virginia.summary: Loving v. Virginia, 388 U.S. 1 (1967), was a landmark civil rights decision of the United States Supreme Court in which the Court ruled that laws banning interracial marriage violated the Equal Protection Clause and the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. The case was brought by Richard Loving, a white man, and his wife Mildred Loving, a woman of African and Native American (Rappahannock) descent. The Lovings had married in Washington, D.C., in 1958, and were subsequently sentenced to a year in prison in their home state of Virginia under that state's Racial Integrity Act of 1924, which criminalized marriage between people classified as white and people classified as colored. The Supreme Court's unanimous decision on June 12, 1967, written by Chief Justice Earl Warren, declared anti-miscegenation laws unconstitutional and struck them down in the 16 states that still had such laws on the books at the time. The decision came after thirteen years of marriage and is widely regarded as a foundational ruling for marriage equality in the United States; the date June 12 is celebrated annually as Loving Day.

Beyond Vietnam.wikipedia_title: Beyond Vietnam: A Time to Break Silence
Beyond Vietnam.description: April 1967 anti-Vietnam War speech delivered by Martin Luther King Jr. at Riverside Church in New York City
Beyond Vietnam.summary: Beyond Vietnam: A Time to Break Silence was a speech delivered by Martin Luther King Jr. at Riverside Church in New York City on April 4, 1967, exactly one year before his assassination. The speech, given to a meeting of Clergy and Laymen Concerned about Vietnam, was King's most public and most controversial denouncement of the Vietnam War. King argued that the United States was in Vietnam 'to occupy it as an American colony,' calling the U.S. government 'the greatest purveyor of violence in the world today.' The speech connected the civil rights movement to the anti-war movement, arguing that the same forces of racism and economic injustice driving the war abroad were driving poverty and oppression at home, and that the war was diverting resources from anti-poverty programs at home. The speech was sharply criticized by mainstream civil rights organizations including the NAACP, by President Lyndon B. Johnson, and by 168 newspapers. King's break with Johnson over Vietnam is widely seen as a turning point that aligned the civil rights movement with the broader 1960s anti-war movement and broadened King's analysis from racial segregation to a triple critique of racism, militarism, and economic exploitation. The Beyond Vietnam speech is significant in the corpus of oral histories from this period because interviewees frequently reference their own decisions about whether to speak out against the war, often citing King's example.

United Farm Workers.description: American labor union founded by Cesar Chavez and Dolores Huerta in 1962 to organize migrant farm workers, primarily Mexican-Americans, in California
United Farm Workers.summary: The United Farm Workers of America (UFW) is a labor union for farm workers in the United States, founded by the merger of two predecessor organizations in 1966: the National Farm Workers Association (NFWA) led by Cesar Chavez and Dolores Huerta, and the Agricultural Workers Organizing Committee (AWOC), led by Larry Itliong, Philip Vera Cruz, and Pete Velasco. The NFWA had been founded in 1962 in Delano, California, by Chavez and Huerta, who had previously worked together at the Community Service Organization. The union came to national prominence during the 1965-1970 Delano grape strike and the subsequent grape boycott, which Chavez led using nonviolent civil disobedience tactics modeled directly on those of the African-American civil rights movement and on Mahatma Gandhi's principles of nonviolent resistance. Chavez became a friend and ally of Martin Luther King Jr.; the two corresponded warmly but never met in person. The 1968 Delano grape boycott succeeded in 1970 when 26 California grape growers signed contracts recognizing the UFW. The UFW's grape boycott is considered one of the most successful boycotts in American labor history and broadened the civil rights movement's framing to include Chicano and Mexican-American labor justice. Cesar Chavez became one of the most prominent Mexican-American civil rights figures of the 20th century; he was posthumously awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1994. Dolores Huerta, a co-founder of the UFW and the architect of the slogan 'Si Se Puede' (Yes We Can), remains an active organizer and was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2012.

Ella Baker.description: African-American civil rights and human rights activist (1903-1986), foundational organizer of SCLC and SNCC
Ella Baker.summary: Ella Josephine Baker (December 13, 1903 - December 13, 1986) was an African-American civil rights and human rights activist whose career spanned more than five decades. She worked alongside W.E.B. Du Bois, Thurgood Marshall, Martin Luther King Jr., and Rosa Parks. She held leadership roles in the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP), the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC), and the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC). Baker is best known for her 1960 organizing role at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina, where she convened the founding meeting of SNCC and insisted, against the preferences of older SCLC leaders, that the new organization remain independent and youth-led rather than becoming an SCLC youth wing. Her philosophy of 'participatory democracy' and group-centered leadership, encapsulated in her dictum that 'strong people don't need strong leaders,' shaped SNCC's organizing model and through it the broader civil rights movement. She mentored a generation of younger activists including Diane Nash, Stokely Carmichael, John Lewis, Bob Moses, and Bernice Johnson Reagon. In 1964 she helped establish the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP). She was a tireless behind-the-scenes organizer whose contributions were often overshadowed by the male preachers of the SCLC during her lifetime but who is now widely recognized as one of the most important Movement figures of the 20th century.

Bayard Rustin.description: American civil rights leader (1912-1987), chief organizer of the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom
Bayard Rustin.summary: Bayard Rustin (March 17, 1912 - August 24, 1987) was an American civil rights, gay rights, and labor rights activist. He worked with A. Philip Randolph on the 1941 March on Washington Movement and was a key adviser to Martin Luther King Jr. on Gandhian nonviolent strategy in the early years of the Montgomery bus boycott. Rustin's most significant single contribution was as chief organizer of the August 28, 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, where King delivered his 'I Have a Dream' speech; Rustin assembled the logistics for the largest civil rights demonstration in U.S. history to that point and produced its program of speakers. As an openly gay man at a time when homosexuality was criminalized in most U.S. states, Rustin was repeatedly marginalized within the Movement -- he was forced out of his role with Randolph in 1953 after a Pasadena morals arrest, and segregationist Senator Strom Thurmond denounced him on the floor of the U.S. Senate before the March on Washington in an attempt to discredit the event by association. Despite this marginalization, Rustin remained a central strategist of the movement and a foundational figure in linking civil rights to economic justice and to the international labor movement. He was posthumously awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Barack Obama in 2013.

Stokely Carmichael.description: Trinidadian-American activist (1941-1998), SNCC chairman 1966-1967, originator of the Black Power slogan
Stokely Carmichael.summary: Stokely Carmichael (later known as Kwame Ture; June 29, 1941 - November 15, 1998) was a Trinidadian-American activist who played a major role in the civil rights movement and the global Pan-African movement. He participated in the Freedom Rides of 1961 (at age 19) and worked extensively as a Mississippi field secretary for the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) on voter registration during Freedom Summer 1964. He was elected SNCC chairman in May 1966, succeeding John Lewis. Carmichael is most widely associated with the slogan 'Black Power,' which he used in a June 16, 1966 speech in Greenwood, Mississippi during the Meredith March Against Fear; the slogan signaled a turn in segments of the civil rights movement away from the integrationist nonviolence associated with the SCLC and toward Black self-determination, economic independence, and political autonomy. Carmichael was honorary prime minister of the Black Panther Party briefly in 1968 before breaking with the Panthers over ideological differences. He left the United States for Guinea in 1969, changed his name to Kwame Ture in 1978 (honoring Ghanaian president Kwame Nkrumah and Guinean president Ahmed Sekou Toure), and spent the rest of his life as a Pan-African organizer with the All-African People's Revolutionary Party. He died of prostate cancer in Conakry, Guinea, in 1998.

Bob Moses.description: American civil rights activist and educator (1935-2021), SNCC field secretary who led Mississippi voter registration
Bob Moses.summary: Robert Parris 'Bob' Moses (January 23, 1935 - July 25, 2021) was an American civil rights activist and educator known for his work as a field secretary for the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) on voter education and registration in Mississippi during the early 1960s, and as a co-founder of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party in 1964. Moses was a Harvard-trained mathematician (M.A. in philosophy from Harvard, 1957) who left a teaching position in New York to join the Movement; Ella Baker recruited him to SNCC. Beginning in 1961 in McComb, Mississippi, Moses led some of the most dangerous voter-registration work of the era, in a state where Black voter registration had been suppressed for nearly a century and where local white authorities and the Citizens' Council responded to organizing with arrests, beatings, and assassinations. He was a principal architect of the 1964 Mississippi Summer Project (Freedom Summer) and of the MFDP's challenge to the all-white Mississippi delegation at the 1964 Democratic National Convention. After the late-1960s breakdown of the civil rights coalition and his disillusionment with the limits of liberal Democratic politics, Moses spent the 1970s in Tanzania teaching mathematics. He returned to the U.S. in 1976 and founded the Algebra Project in 1982, applying his organizing experience to the project of making algebra accessible to Black and low-income students as a civil-rights issue for the post-segregation era. The Algebra Project framed mathematical literacy as the modern equivalent of the right to vote and operated in school districts across the country until his death in 2021.

John Lewis.description: American civil rights leader and politician (1940-2020), SNCC chairman 1963-1966, longtime U.S. Representative from Georgia's 5th district
John Lewis.summary: John Robert Lewis (February 21, 1940 - July 17, 2020) was an American civil rights activist and politician. The son of sharecroppers, he was born in Troy, Alabama, and as a young man led the Nashville student sit-in movement and was a leader of the 1961 Freedom Rides. He served as chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) from 1963 to 1966, and at age 23 was the youngest of the six major speakers at the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom (his prepared remarks were edited down at the last minute under pressure from other movement leaders who considered the original too confrontational). On March 7, 1965 -- the day that became known as Bloody Sunday -- Lewis was one of the leaders of the planned Selma to Montgomery march, and was beaten by Alabama state troopers at the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma; his skull was fractured. The televised images of the attack contributed directly to the passage of the Voting Rights Act of 1965 five months later. Lewis was arrested more than forty times during the civil rights years. After leaving SNCC in 1966 over disagreements with the Black Power turn under Stokely Carmichael, he later served on the Atlanta City Council and was elected to represent Georgia's 5th congressional district in 1986, a seat he held until his death from pancreatic cancer in 2020. He was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Barack Obama in 2011 and lay in state in the U.S. Capitol Rotunda upon his death, the first Black lawmaker to receive that honor.

Fannie Lou Hamer.description: American civil rights and voting rights activist (1917-1977), co-founder of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party
Fannie Lou Hamer.summary: Fannie Lou Hamer (October 6, 1917 - March 14, 1977) was an American voting and civil rights activist and community organizer. Born the youngest of twenty children in Montgomery County, Mississippi, to sharecropper parents, she worked on a Mississippi Delta plantation from age six. In August 1962, at age 44, she attended a Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) meeting in Ruleville and decided to attempt to register to vote -- an act for which she was fired from the plantation that day and threatened with violence. In June 1963, while returning from a citizenship education workshop, she and other activists were arrested in Winona, Mississippi, and Hamer was severely beaten in the jail, suffering permanent kidney damage. She became a SNCC field secretary and a leading voice of the Mississippi voter-registration movement. In 1964 she co-founded the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP) with Bob Moses, Aaron Henry, Ed King, Lawrence Guyot, and Annie Devine, and delivered nationally-televised testimony before the Credentials Committee of the 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City describing the beating she received in the Winona jail for trying to register to vote -- testimony that President Lyndon B. Johnson attempted to block from live coverage by calling an impromptu press conference at the same time. Her phrase 'I am sick and tired of being sick and tired' (from a 1964 speech) became one of the most quoted formulations of the movement. She ran for Congress from Mississippi's 2nd district in 1964 and 1965 (unsuccessfully under MFDP), and continued to work on poverty programs and the Freedom Farm Cooperative in the Mississippi Delta until her death from breast cancer in 1977.

Diane Nash.description: American civil rights activist (born 1938), Nashville sit-in leader and SNCC co-founder
Diane Nash.summary: Diane Nash (born May 15, 1938) is an American civil rights activist who played a leading role in the Nashville student sit-in movement of 1960, the 1961 Freedom Rides, and the 1965 Selma voting rights movement. Born in Chicago and educated at Howard University and Fisk University in Nashville, Nash emerged in early 1960 as a strategist of the Nashville student sit-in campaign, which used the workshops James Lawson taught in nonviolent direct action to organize coordinated sit-ins at downtown Nashville segregated lunch counters; the Nashville campaign became a model for the sit-in movement that swept the South in 1960. She was one of the founding student organizers of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) at Shaw University in April 1960. In May 1961, when the original CORE-organized Freedom Riders were brutally attacked in Anniston and Birmingham and CORE leadership decided to end the rides, Nash organized the Nashville student volunteers who continued the rides, arguing that allowing violence to stop the movement would establish a fatal precedent. She and her husband James Bevel later developed and presented to King and the SCLC the strategic plan for the Selma voting rights campaign that culminated in Bloody Sunday and the Voting Rights Act. Nash is one of the few publicly visible female strategists of the early movement; she has been awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom (2023) and the Distinguished American Award.

Septima Clark.description: American educator and civil rights activist (1898-1987), architect of the Citizenship Schools that taught literacy and voter education across the Deep South
Septima Clark.summary: Septima Poinsette Clark (May 3, 1898 - December 15, 1987) was an American educator and civil rights activist whose work creating and running the Citizenship Schools program -- first at the Highlander Folk School in Tennessee in 1957, then later under the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) when Highlander's charter was revoked in 1961 -- taught literacy, voter education, and basic civics to tens of thousands of Black adults across the Deep South, equipping them to pass the discriminatory literacy tests Southern states used to bar Black voter registration. Clark was born in Charleston, South Carolina, the daughter of a former slave, and trained as a teacher; she lost her South Carolina teaching certification in 1956 for refusing to renounce her NAACP membership. Myles Horton recruited her to Highlander, where she developed the Citizenship Schools curriculum modeled on the Sea Islands literacy classes she had taught with her cousin Bernice Robinson. After Tennessee shut Highlander down in 1961, Clark moved the program to the SCLC, where Andrew Young administered it. By the time the Citizenship Schools program ended in 1970, more than 10,000 teachers had been trained and an estimated 25,000 to 100,000 Black adults had passed through its classes -- a quantitatively enormous contribution to the voter-registration drives that the more publicly visible Movement campaigns relied on. Martin Luther King Jr. called Clark 'the architect of the civil rights movement' and 'the queen mother of the movement.' She was the first woman elected to the SCLC's executive board (in 1956) and received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from Jimmy Carter in 1979.

James Lawson.description: American Methodist minister and civil rights activist (1928-2024), theological architect of nonviolent direct action in the American Movement
James Lawson.summary: James Morris Lawson Jr. (September 22, 1928 - June 9, 2024) was an American Methodist minister and a leading theorist and tactician of nonviolent direct action in the American civil rights movement. Lawson studied Mahatma Gandhi's principles of nonviolent resistance during three years in India in the 1950s, then returned to the United States as a Methodist missionary and enrolled in Vanderbilt University Divinity School in Nashville, where he began conducting workshops on the philosophy and practice of nonviolent direct action for Black college students in the city. The workshops were attended by Diane Nash, John Lewis, James Bevel, Bernard Lafayette, Marion Barry, C.T. Vivian, and other future Movement leaders, and trained them in the techniques (role-playing how to absorb verbal and physical abuse without retaliating, careful preparation of dress and demeanor, coordinated mass arrests) that the Nashville student sit-in movement of February 1960 then deployed at downtown Nashville lunch counters. Lawson was expelled from Vanderbilt in March 1960 for his civil rights organizing -- an act of academic disciplining that sparked national protest and that Vanderbilt formally apologized for and revoked decades later, ultimately awarding Lawson an honorary doctorate. Martin Luther King Jr. called Lawson 'the leading nonviolent theorist in the world.' Lawson played key strategic roles in the 1961 Freedom Rides, the 1968 Memphis sanitation workers' strike (he invited King to Memphis), and dozens of other Movement campaigns. He pastored Centenary United Methodist Church in Memphis and later Holman United Methodist Church in Los Angeles, and remained an active organizer until his death in 2024 at age 95.

Rosa Parks.description: American civil rights activist (1913-2005), arrested in December 1955 for refusing to give up her seat on a Montgomery bus, catalyzing the Montgomery bus boycott
Rosa Parks.summary: Rosa Louise McCauley Parks (February 4, 1913 - October 24, 2005) was an American civil rights activist whose refusal to give up her seat to a white passenger on a segregated Montgomery, Alabama, city bus on December 1, 1955, sparked the Montgomery bus boycott that propelled Martin Luther King Jr. to national prominence and launched the modern phase of the civil rights movement. Parks was not the first Black woman to be arrested for refusing to surrender her seat (Claudette Colvin and others had done the same months earlier), but the NAACP and the Montgomery Improvement Association chose her arrest as the test case because of her impeccable reputation, her training, and her position as secretary of the Montgomery NAACP chapter -- a role she had held since 1943. Before the bus arrest she had attended a 1955 workshop at the Highlander Folk School on integrating public schools, and she had investigated cases of racial violence in Alabama including the rape of Recy Taylor. After the bus boycott Parks and her husband Raymond were forced out of Montgomery by death threats and financial pressure (both lost their jobs); they relocated to Detroit in 1957, where she worked for U.S. Representative John Conyers Jr. from 1965 to 1988 and continued civil rights activism for the rest of her life. She co-founded the Rosa and Raymond Parks Institute for Self Development in 1987. She received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1996, the Congressional Gold Medal in 1999, and upon her death in 2005 became the first woman and the second African American to lie in honor in the U.S. Capitol Rotunda.

Thurgood Marshall.description: American civil rights lawyer and U.S. Supreme Court Justice (1908-1993), lead counsel in Brown v. Board of Education and first Black justice on the Supreme Court
Thurgood Marshall.summary: Thurgood Marshall (July 2, 1908 - January 24, 1993) was an American civil rights lawyer and jurist who served as an Associate Justice of the U.S. Supreme Court from 1967 to 1991. Before his elevation to the Court, Marshall built and led the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund's litigation strategy against state-sanctioned segregation -- a multi-decade legal campaign in which he argued 32 cases before the U.S. Supreme Court and won 29 of them, the highest record of any non-government litigant in the Court's history. His most consequential victory was Brown v. Board of Education (1954), in which the Court unanimously held that state-sponsored school segregation was unconstitutional, overturning the 'separate but equal' doctrine that Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) had established. Other landmark Marshall victories included Smith v. Allwright (1944, ending the all-white Democratic primary in Texas), Shelley v. Kraemer (1948, prohibiting state enforcement of racially restrictive housing covenants), and Sweatt v. Painter (1950, integrating the University of Texas Law School). Born in Baltimore and educated at Lincoln University and Howard University School of Law (he was denied admission to the University of Maryland Law School because of his race, an injustice he later litigated as Murray v. Pearson in 1936), Marshall served as a federal judge on the Second Circuit and then as U.S. Solicitor General before President Lyndon B. Johnson nominated him to the Supreme Court in 1967. On the Court he was a consistent voice for civil rights, criminal defendants' rights, and the abolition of capital punishment. He retired in 1991 and was succeeded by Justice Clarence Thomas.

Pauli Murray.description: American lawyer, theologian, poet, and civil rights activist (1910-1985), author of the 1944 'States' Laws on Race and Color' that became the legal blueprint for Brown v. Board, co-founder of NOW, first Black woman ordained as an Episcopal priest
Pauli Murray.summary: Anna Pauline 'Pauli' Murray (November 20, 1910 - July 1, 1985) was an American lawyer, theologian, poet, and civil rights activist whose intellectual contributions across law, gender theory, and religious thought were decades ahead of the institutions that eventually adopted them. Murray was born in Baltimore, raised in Durham, North Carolina, after her mother's death, and graduated from Hunter College in 1933 -- one of four African American students in a class of 247. Denied admission to the University of North Carolina graduate program in 1938 because of her race (a rejection she challenged in correspondence with university president Frank Porter Graham and President Franklin D. Roosevelt), Murray instead graduated first in her class at Howard University School of Law in 1944. Her senior thesis, 'Should the Civil Rights Cases and Plessy v. Ferguson Be Overruled?', argued that 'separate but equal' was inherently unequal and should be challenged head-on -- the legal strategy NAACP attorneys had previously avoided in favor of incremental challenges to specific inequalities. Thurgood Marshall called Murray's later 1951 book 'States' Laws on Race and Color' -- a 746-page compilation of every American segregation statute then in force -- 'the Bible' for the NAACP Legal Defense Fund's Brown v. Board of Education litigation. In 1965 Murray earned a JSD from Yale Law School, the first African American to receive that degree from Yale; her dissertation on the Fourteenth Amendment's Equal Protection Clause and gender discrimination, written with Mary Eastwood, was cited by Ruth Bader Ginsburg in the brief for Reed v. Reed (1971), the first Supreme Court case to strike down a state law on gender-discrimination grounds. Murray coined the term 'Jane Crow' to describe the intersectional discrimination Black women faced -- a conceptual framework Kimberle Crenshaw later named 'intersectionality' in 1989. Murray co-founded the National Organization for Women (NOW) in 1966 with Betty Friedan and others. In 1977, at age 66, she became the first African American woman ordained as an Episcopal priest. Murray is now understood by many scholars to have been a transgender or non-binary person -- she sought hormone therapy in the 1930s and 1940s and wrote extensively about her gender identity in private papers, though the contemporary vocabulary was not available to her. She was canonized as an Episcopal saint in 2012.

Ida B. Wells.description: American investigative journalist, educator, and civil rights pioneer (1862-1931), co-founder of the NAACP, foundational figure in the anti-lynching movement
Ida B. Wells.summary: Ida Bell Wells-Barnett (July 16, 1862 - March 25, 1931) was an American investigative journalist, educator, and civil rights pioneer who was the leading anti-lynching crusader of the late 19th and early 20th centuries and a co-founder of both the NAACP (1909) and the Alpha Suffrage Club (1913, the first Black women's suffrage organization). Born into slavery in Holly Springs, Mississippi, six months before the Emancipation Proclamation, Wells was orphaned at sixteen when both parents and a sibling died in the 1878 yellow fever epidemic; she took a teaching position to keep her younger siblings together and out of foster care. In 1884, she sued the Chesapeake, Ohio and Southwestern Railroad after a conductor forcibly removed her from a first-class ladies' car to which she had a paid ticket -- a case she initially won at the local level (a $500 judgment) before the Tennessee Supreme Court reversed in 1887, predating Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) by nine years as an early Black legal challenge to railway segregation. Her anti-lynching work began after the March 9, 1892, lynching of her close friend Thomas Moss, a grocer in Memphis, along with William Stewart and Calvin McDowell -- killed by a white mob over competition with a white-owned grocery. Wells's investigative reporting in 'Free Speech' (the Memphis paper she co-owned) and her 1892 pamphlet 'Southern Horrors: Lynch Law in All Its Phases' systematically documented and rebutted the standard pretexts white communities offered to justify lynchings, demonstrating through case-by-case research that the majority of lynchings were not in response to claims of rape but to economic competition, political organizing, and challenges to white supremacy. Her 'Free Speech' offices were destroyed by a white mob in May 1892 and she was exiled from Memphis under death threats; she continued her anti-lynching work from Chicago. Her 1895 pamphlet 'A Red Record' compiled lynching statistics from 1892-1894. Wells was one of two African American women among the 60 founding signers of the NAACP's Call in 1909, though her more militant anti-lynching politics led to tensions with the male leadership and she was effectively pushed out of the NAACP's executive committee. She received the Pulitzer Prize posthumously in 2020 'for her outstanding and courageous reporting on the horrific and vicious violence against African Americans during the era of lynching.'

Lyndon B. Johnson.description: 36th president of the United States (1908-1973), signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965, architect of the Great Society and War on Poverty
Lyndon B. Johnson.summary: Lyndon Baines Johnson (August 27, 1908 - January 22, 1973) was the 36th president of the United States, serving from November 22, 1963, when he was sworn in aboard Air Force One after the assassination of John F. Kennedy, until January 20, 1969. Johnson's tenure produced the most consequential civil rights legislation of the 20th century: the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (signed July 2, 1964, prohibiting discrimination on the basis of race, color, religion, sex, or national origin in public accommodations and employment and ending Jim Crow segregation in public facilities), the Voting Rights Act of 1965 (signed August 6, 1965, banning literacy tests and authorizing federal oversight of voter registration in jurisdictions with a history of discrimination -- the bill passed in the wake of Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge in Selma), and the Fair Housing Act / Civil Rights Act of 1968 (signed April 11, 1968, prohibiting discrimination in housing sale, rental, and financing, passed seven days after the assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.). Johnson also launched the War on Poverty (Economic Opportunity Act of 1964 creating Job Corps, VISTA, Head Start, and the Office of Economic Opportunity) and the Great Society legislative program including Medicare and Medicaid (1965), the Elementary and Secondary Education Act (1965), the Higher Education Act (1965), and the Immigration and Nationality Act (1965, ending the national-origin quota system). Johnson was a Texas Democrat who had voted against earlier civil rights bills as a senator in the 1940s and 1950s but, as president, used his legislative skill (developed during 12 years as Senate Majority Leader, 1955-1961) to push the 1964 and 1965 laws through over Southern Democratic filibuster resistance -- famously telling aide Bill Moyers after signing the 1964 Act that 'we have lost the South for a generation.' His decision to escalate U.S. involvement in the Vietnam War crippled his domestic agenda and his civil rights legacy; he declined to seek re-election in 1968. King's April 4, 1967, 'Beyond Vietnam' speech at Riverside Church publicly criticized Johnson's Vietnam policy and damaged the King-Johnson relationship for the year leading to King's assassination.

W.E.B. Du Bois.description: American sociologist, historian, and civil rights activist (1868-1963), co-founder of the NAACP, first African American to earn a Harvard doctorate, foundational intellectual of the civil rights movement
W.E.B. Du Bois.summary: William Edward Burghardt Du Bois (February 23, 1868 - August 27, 1963) was an American sociologist, historian, philosopher, and civil rights activist whose intellectual and organizing work spanning the 1890s through the early 1960s laid the intellectual foundation for the modern American civil rights movement. Du Bois was the first African American to earn a doctorate from Harvard University (PhD in history, 1895), where he later studied under William James, George Santayana, and Josiah Royce; he also studied at the University of Berlin. His foundational works include 'The Suppression of the African Slave Trade to the United States of America, 1638-1870' (his 1896 Harvard dissertation, published as the first volume of the Harvard Historical Studies series), 'The Philadelphia Negro' (1899, the first sociological case study of an African-American community), 'The Souls of Black Folk' (1903, a foundational text articulating concepts of double-consciousness and the color line that 'the problem of the twentieth century is the problem of the color line'), and 'Black Reconstruction in America 1860-1880' (1935, the revisionist history that overturned the Dunning School's apologetic interpretation of Reconstruction and established the modern historiographical consensus). Du Bois publicly opposed Booker T. Washington's accommodationist 'Atlanta Compromise' in his 1903 essay 'Of Mr. Booker T. Washington and Others', arguing for full political and civil rights for African Americans rather than vocational education and gradual economic advancement. He co-founded the Niagara Movement in 1905 with William Monroe Trotter, which led to the founding of the NAACP in 1909-1910, where Du Bois served as the founding editor of its monthly magazine 'The Crisis' from 1910 to 1934. Disillusioned with the United States and the slow pace of civil rights gains, Du Bois renounced his U.S. citizenship and emigrated to Ghana in 1961 at the invitation of President Kwame Nkrumah, where he began work on the Encyclopedia Africana and where he died on August 27, 1963 -- one day before the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, where Roy Wilkins announced his death to the assembled crowd.

James Farmer.description: American civil rights leader (1920-1999), co-founder and national director of the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE), architect of the 1961 Freedom Rides
James Farmer.summary: James Leonard Farmer Jr. (January 12, 1920 - July 9, 1999) was an American civil rights leader and one of the 'Big Six' organizers of the civil rights movement of the 1960s, best known as the co-founder of the Congress of Racial Equality (CORE) in 1942 and as the national director who conceived and organized the 1961 Freedom Rides. Born in Marshall, Texas, the son of a Methodist minister and theology professor, Farmer was a child prodigy who entered Wiley College at age 14 and graduated at 18, then earned a divinity degree from Howard University in 1941 (he refused ordination because he could not in conscience pastor a segregated church). Together with George Houser, Bernice Fisher, and others at the Fellowship of Reconciliation, he co-founded the Committee of Racial Equality in Chicago in 1942 -- the organization that became the Congress of Racial Equality -- which developed nonviolent direct-action techniques against segregation more than a decade before the Montgomery bus boycott. Farmer was national director of CORE from 1961 to 1966. The 1961 Freedom Rides -- thirteen interracial riders boarding interstate buses from Washington D.C. into the segregated South to test the Boynton v. Virginia (1960) Supreme Court ruling that had outlawed segregation in interstate transportation facilities -- were his strategic conception; the violence the riders met at Anniston and Birmingham produced the federal intervention that forced the Interstate Commerce Commission to enforce desegregation in November 1961. Farmer was a member of the Big Six who organized the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom (though he was in a Louisiana jail at the time of the March and could not attend). He served as Assistant Secretary of Health, Education, and Welfare under President Nixon from 1969 to 1970, an appointment that drew criticism from some Movement colleagues. Farmer was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Bill Clinton in 1998. He died in Fredericksburg, Virginia, in 1999 at age 79, having lost both legs to diabetes complications and his eyesight in his final years.

Whitney Young.description: American civil rights leader (1921-1971), executive director of the National Urban League from 1961 to 1971, architect of corporate and government partnerships for economic justice
Whitney Young.summary: Whitney Moore Young Jr. (July 31, 1921 - March 11, 1971) was an American civil rights leader who served as executive director of the National Urban League from 1961 until his death in 1971, and was one of the 'Big Six' leaders of the civil rights movement of the 1960s. Young was born in Lincoln Ridge, Kentucky, the son of a college president and a postmaster -- one of the few African Americans in that role in the segregated South -- and graduated from Kentucky State College in 1941. He earned a master's degree in social work from the University of Minnesota in 1947 and joined the Omaha Urban League the same year, rising through Urban League posts in Saint Paul and Omaha and serving as dean of the Atlanta University School of Social Work from 1954 to 1960 before being named the League's national executive director in 1961. Young's strategic posture was distinctive within the Big Six: rather than legal litigation (Wilkins's NAACP) or direct-action protest (King's SCLC, Lewis's SNCC, Farmer's CORE), Young focused on building corporate and government partnerships to address economic inequality, arguing that civil rights without jobs and economic opportunity were hollow. He authored 'To Be Equal' (1964) proposing a 'Marshall Plan' for Black Americans -- a domestic-policy program of compensatory funding for African-American education, jobs, housing, and health -- ideas that influenced President Lyndon B. Johnson's War on Poverty programs. Young helped organize the 1963 March on Washington and was one of the platform speakers. He worked closely with both the Kennedy and Johnson administrations and with Fortune 500 CEOs, opening private-sector hiring and promotion paths for African Americans that had been closed for generations. Young drowned in Lagos, Nigeria, on March 11, 1971, at age 49, while attending the African-American Dialogue conference. President Richard Nixon delivered the eulogy at his funeral and awarded him a posthumous Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1969 (with the formal ceremony occurring later); LBJ had already awarded him the Medal in 1968.

Roy Wilkins.description: American civil rights leader (1901-1981), executive director of the NAACP from 1955 to 1977, principal voice of the NAACP's legalist strategy through the entire civil rights era
Roy Wilkins.summary: Roy Ottoway Wilkins (August 30, 1901 - September 8, 1981) was an American civil rights leader who served as executive secretary (1955-1964) and then executive director (1964-1977) of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) during the most consequential decades of the civil rights movement. Born in St. Louis, Missouri, and raised in St. Paul, Minnesota, Wilkins graduated from the University of Minnesota in 1923 and worked as a journalist for the Kansas City Call before joining the NAACP national staff in 1931 as assistant secretary to Walter F. White. He took over the NAACP's flagship magazine 'The Crisis' from W.E.B. Du Bois in 1934. As the NAACP's chief executive during the King-era Movement, Wilkins represented the organization's legalist strategic tradition -- using the courts (Brown v. Board of Education, Loving v. Virginia, dozens of school desegregation cases) and Washington lobbying to achieve civil rights gains -- a posture that sometimes brought him into tension with the direct-action wing led by King at the SCLC and the more radical organizers at SNCC and CORE. He was a member of the 'Big Six' civil rights leadership council that organized the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom and was one of the six speakers on the platform that day. Wilkins met with every U.S. president from Franklin D. Roosevelt to Jimmy Carter and was a principal advocate for the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and the Voting Rights Act of 1965. President Lyndon B. Johnson awarded him the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1969. After his retirement from the NAACP in 1977 he remained active in civil rights advocacy until his death from kidney failure in 1981.

Dorothy Height.description: American civil rights and women's rights activist (1912-2010), president of the National Council of Negro Women from 1957 to 1997, strategic figure in civil rights coalition-building
Dorothy Height.summary: Dorothy Irene Height (March 24, 1912 - April 20, 2010) was an American civil rights and women's rights activist whose 40-year presidency of the National Council of Negro Women (NCNW, 1957-1997) and decades of behind-the-scenes coalition-building work made her one of the most consequential strategic figures of the civil rights movement -- a contribution that was systematically under-credited at the time because of the gendered exclusion of women leaders from public-facing roles in the Movement coalition. Height was born in Richmond, Virginia, grew up in Rankin, Pennsylvania, and earned bachelor's and master's degrees from New York University. She joined the YWCA national staff in 1944 and became director of its Department for the Integration of Racial Justice, which led the YWCA's transition to a fully racially integrated organization. As president of NCNW she organized the 'Wednesdays in Mississippi' interracial dialogue project (1964-1965), funded and ran 'Operation Big Vote' voter-registration drives, founded the Black Family Reunion celebrations in 1986, and served as a key strategist in the planning of the 1963 March on Washington -- she was the only woman seated on the platform with the male organizers (King, John Lewis, A. Philip Randolph, Roy Wilkins, Whitney Young, Bayard Rustin) and was not allowed to speak from the podium, an exclusion she later described as 'a sore point' that the male leadership refused to discuss. Height was a member of the 'Big Six' civil rights leadership council (Wilkins, Young, King, Randolph, Lewis, Farmer) even though she was rarely named in contemporary press as such. She was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by Bill Clinton in 1994 and the Congressional Gold Medal by George W. Bush in 2004. President Barack Obama delivered her eulogy in 2010, calling her 'the godmother of the civil rights movement and a hero to so many Americans.'

Andrew Young.description: American civil rights activist and politician (born 1932), SCLC executive director under MLK, later U.S. ambassador to the United Nations and mayor of Atlanta
Andrew Young.summary: Andrew Jackson Young Jr. (born March 12, 1932) is an American politician, diplomat, and civil rights activist who served as executive director of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC) under Martin Luther King Jr. from 1964 to 1968, and later as U.S. ambassador to the United Nations (1977-1979) under President Jimmy Carter and as the 55th mayor of Atlanta (1982-1990). Young trained as a Congregationalist minister at Hartford Theological Seminary and entered Movement work through the National Council of Churches' field staff before joining the SCLC in 1961, where he ran the Citizenship Schools program that Septima Clark had developed at Highlander Folk School. As SCLC's principal administrator he played central roles in the Birmingham campaign (1963), the St. Augustine campaign (1964), the Selma to Montgomery marches (1965), and the Chicago Freedom Movement (1966), and was with King at the Lorraine Motel in Memphis on April 4, 1968, when King was assassinated. After leaving SCLC, Young was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives from Georgia's 5th congressional district in 1972 (the first Black congressman elected from Georgia since Reconstruction), where he served until Carter appointed him UN ambassador in 1977. His UN tenure ended in August 1979 after he held unauthorized meetings with representatives of the Palestine Liberation Organization in violation of standing U.S. policy. As Atlanta mayor he attracted major corporate investment to the city and was a key figure in Atlanta's successful 1996 Olympic bid. Young was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom by President Carter in 1981, and continued public-service work into his nineties through the Andrew Young Foundation.

Ralph Abernathy.description: American Baptist minister and civil rights activist (1926-1990), Martin Luther King Jr.'s closest associate, co-founder and second president of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference
Ralph Abernathy.summary: Ralph David Abernathy (March 11, 1926 - April 17, 1990) was an American civil rights activist and Baptist minister who was Martin Luther King Jr.'s closest friend and most consistent co-organizer across the entire arc of the King-era Movement. Abernathy pastored First Baptist Church in Montgomery, Alabama, where he met King in 1955 shortly after King arrived at Dexter Avenue Baptist Church. When Rosa Parks was arrested on December 1, 1955, Abernathy was the central figure in the Montgomery Improvement Association's formation -- it was Abernathy who recommended that the new association elect King as its president, and the two pastors led the 381-day Montgomery bus boycott together. In January 1957 they co-founded the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (SCLC); Abernathy was its first secretary-treasurer and, after King's assassination on April 4, 1968, became SCLC's second president. He led the Poor People's Campaign that King had been planning at the time of his death, organizing the Resurrection City encampment on the National Mall in Washington from May to June 1968.
