Bob Kiley.description: NSA president (1958-59) → CIA International Organizations Division → Richard Helms's Special Assistant → MBTA Boston chairman (1975-79) → NYC MTA chairman (1983-90) → London Transport Commissioner (2001-06).
Bob Kiley.summary: Bob Kiley is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: NSA president (1958-59) → CIA International Organizations Division → Richard Helms's Special Assistant → MBTA Boston chairman (1975-79) → NYC MTA chairman (1983-90) → London Transport Commissioner (2001-06). Why it belongs: Kiley is the single most-cited NSA-to-CIA pipeline figure across the corpus. McNichols's first-person account of learning post-facto that the NSA international circuit was a CIA front ("we didn't realize it was a CIA front") is canonical testimony on the 1967 Ramparts magazine revelations. Kiley's career arc from NSA leadership to CIA to Helms Special Assistant is the concrete institutional example of the CIA-civil-rights-student-network entanglement. No corpus entry exists for the NSA-CIA infiltration history. Corpus already covers Freedom Summer, MFDP, and SNCC; the NSA-CIA dimension is a significant gap. Transcript evidence: "The head of the network was a guy named Bob Kiley, who'd been president of National Student Association... He became... Richard Helms Special Assistant... we didn't realize it was a CIA front." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 118.

Bobby Rush.summary: Bobby Rush is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Illinois BPP co-founder; US Representative 1993-2023 (IL-1) Why it belongs: Target of the December 4, 1969 FBI/Chicago PD raid that killed Fred Hampton ("they wanted to kill Bobby Rush, but he wasn't there"). Fred Hampton is already in corpus; Bobby Rush is the co-founder who survived and went on to a 30-year Congressional career. His absence from the corpus creates an asymmetry in the Hampton-related entries. Likely recurring in other Illinois BPP interview transcripts. Transcript evidence: "They start in Chicago. They go in there. They kill Fred Hampton. And they wanted to kill Bobby Rush, but he wasn't there." (Pass 2 tail 1.P2T.9, marked correct) This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 1.

Carter G. Woodson.description: African-American historian, author of The Negro in Our History (1922; 11 editions through 1966), founder of the Association for the Study of Negro Life and History (ASNLH, 1915), founder of Negro History Week (1926 - became Black History M.

Role or context: African-American historian, author of The Negro in Our History (1922; 11 editions through 1966), founder of the Association for the Study of Negro Life and History (ASNLH, 1915), founder of Negro History Week (1926 - became Black History Month 1976), founder of the Journal of Negro History (1916). One of the earliest and most influential systematizers of Black history as an academic and pedagogical discipline. Transcript evidence: Speaker: "the only new book I ever had was the history of the Negro. By Carter G. Woodson. All the other books we had had pages torn out, or marked up." (Whisper-rendered "Cartergy Witson" - corrected high-confidence in Pass 1 #91.4, Pass 2 #91.P2.4). Pass 3 row 91.P3 confirmed The Negro in Our History (1922) as the specific canonical Woodson textbook referenced. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 91.

Charles B. Rangel.description: U.S. Army veteran (9th Infantry Regiment, Korea; Bronze Star at Battle of Kunu-ri, November 1950); later 23-term U.S. Representative from Harlem, New York (1971-2017); long-serving Chairman of the House Ways and Means Committee
Charles B. Rangel.summary: Charles B. Rangel is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: U.S. Army veteran (9th Infantry Regiment, Korea; Bronze Star at Battle of Kunu-ri, November 1950); later 23-term U.S. Representative from Harlem, New York (1971-2017); long-serving Chairman of the House Ways and Means Committee Why it belongs: Rangel served in the same regiment (9th Infantry) and the same Korean War campaign (Battle of Kunu-ri, November 1950) as Becton. He is explicitly referenced in Becton's transcript as a fellow 9th Infantry veteran. His career trajectory - Korean War integration-era combat veteran → foundational Black political figure in New York City → 23-term congressional career - makes him a canonical figure linking Korean War Black military service to post-war Black political leadership. The Adam Clayton Powell Jr. entry (already in corpus) notes that Powell "was defeated in the 1970 Democratic primary by Charles Rangel," confirming Rangel appears in the. Transcript evidence: Pass 1 #71.35, Pass 2 #71.P2.9, #71.P2.42 - confirmed across multiple passes. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 71.

Clayton \"Peg Leg\" Bates (1907-1998) /.wikipedia_title: Clayton "Peg Leg" Bates (1907-1998) / Peg Leg Bates Country Club
Clayton \"Peg Leg\" Bates (1907-1998) /.description: One-legged Black tap dancer who founded the Peg Leg Bates Country Club in Kerhonkson, NY in 1951 - the foundational Black resort in the Catskills, which closed in 1989 as the rise of integrated travel options eliminated the Black resort ma.
Clayton \"Peg Leg\" Bates (1907-1998) /.summary: Clayton "Peg Leg" Bates (1907-1998) / Peg Leg Bates Country Club is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: One-legged Black tap dancer who founded the Peg Leg Bates Country Club in Kerhonkson, NY in 1951 - the foundational Black resort in the Catskills, which closed in 1989 as the rise of integrated travel options eliminated the Black resort market. He is a canonical figure in the "integration closed Black businesses" discourse. Why it belongs: Grinnell's testimony ("Peg Leg Bates had a place in the upper New York... it closed when integration happened") is canonical first-person material on the economic collateral damage of integration on Black cultural institutions - a recurring theme in CRHP transcripts from interviewees of Grinnell's generation. The corpus has no entry for Peg Leg Bates. His name is subject to Whisper degradation ("Peg Lake Bates" in this transcript). An alias list entry would help downstream correction passes in other transcripts that likely reference him. Transcript evidence: "Peg Leg Bates had a place in the upper New York. I can't think of what it's called. People back east know it. And I heard that the black people used to go there and stay overnight and have a good time. And it closed when integration happened." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 48.

David Graham Du Bois.description: Stepson of W.E.B. Du Bois (through Shirley Graham Du Bois); editor of English-language daily in Cairo (Egyptian Gazette, ~1960s-70s); editor-in-chief of The Black Panther newspaper (1972+); novelist (And Bid Him Sing, 1975).
David Graham Du Bois.summary: David Graham Du Bois is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Stepson of W.E.B. Du Bois (through Shirley Graham Du Bois); editor of English-language daily in Cairo (Egyptian Gazette, ~1960s-70s); editor-in-chief of The Black Panther newspaper (1972+); novelist (And Bid Him Sing, 1975). Why it belongs: Hopkins's account of working under David Du Bois at The Black Panther newspaper is the most detailed first-person source on Du Bois in the entire corpus. Multiple canonical facts about Du Bois are corroborated by the transcript: his Cairo editing career, his relationship to W.E.B. Du Bois and Shirley Graham Du Bois, his role as the newspaper's editor-in-chief, and his novel And Bid Him Sing. David Graham Du Bois is distinct from W.E.B. Du Bois (who is already in the corpus at line 300) and warrants his own entry given his pivotal role in BPP media infrastructure and his presence in multiple corpus entries (Hopkins #42, Big Man Howard #35, an. Transcript evidence: Segments 314-322 (corrected): "David Dubois came and became the editor-in-chief of the paper... he had edited the English-speaking daily in Cairo, Egypt for the previous 12 years... he was working on a novel... called, and bid him sing." Eleanor Roosevelt.description: First Lady of the United States (1933-45); foundational New Deal-era civil rights advocate; personal backer of the Farm Security Administration Black-resettlement projects in Fayette and Haywood County TN, Mileston MS, and Lakeview AR

Role or context: First Lady of the United States (1933-45); foundational New Deal-era civil rights advocate; personal backer of the Farm Security Administration Black-resettlement projects in Fayette and Haywood County TN, Mileston MS, and Lakeview AR Why it belongs: Eleanor Roosevelt is a cross-corpus figure referenced in nearly every interview touching 1930s federal New Deal land-ownership programs, the 1939 Daughters of the American Revolution resignation over Marian Anderson's Constitution Hall bar, and the pre-Movement white-ally tradition. Critically, she is absent from the current 140-entry corpus despite being likely the single most canonical white-ally bridge figure between the New Deal era and the Civil Rights Movement. Transcript evidence: Corrected transcript: "Eleanor Roosevelt, you know, was involved [in the WPA/FSA Black land-ownership project in Fayette/Haywood County]." Pass 4 row 124.P4.1 confirms Whisper rendered this as "Illinois Roosevelt" - the Whisper-failure itself signals how frequently she appears in ML-transcribed oral history audio. Role or context: German-Jewish refugee sociologist; Tougaloo College sociology professor; founder of the Tougaloo Social Science Forum, which brought nationally recognized speakers to the college during the segregation era. Additional Pass 7 role notes: Tougaloo College German-Jewish-refugee sociology professor (1947-1983) who taught and mentored civil-rights-movement students through his Social Science Laboratory - a foundational white-ally figure in Mississippi civil-rights solidarity. Why it belongs: A distinctly unusual figure (German-Jewish refugee as foundational HBCU intellectual sponsor) who appears in multiple Tougaloo-related transcripts. Adams-Johnson names "the Berenc skis" (= Borinskis, plural, referring to the Borinski-era sociology professors as a cohort) in two separate transcript passages (Pass 1 #43.18, Pass 2 #43.P2.14). The corrected transcript has "you had the Borinskis, who embrace social change and encouraged us in sociology classes." Borinski appears in multiple corpus entries and anchors the Tougaloo College white-academic network. Transcript evidence: "Dr. Ernst Borinski, of course, Dr. Ernst Borinski. And then the forums that he held, yes, I used to attend those. As a matter of fact, when I went to summer school, he taught me German." Pass 2 #43.P2.14 (Berenc skis → Borinskis); corrected transcript "you had the Borinskis." Harambee Singers.description: Atlanta-based Black women's a cappella ensemble co-founded ca. 1968 by Bernice Johnson Reagon, Jamila Jones, and Matti Casey Pierce; performed Pan-African / Black Power-era repertoire ("Hands Off Nkrumah," "We'll Move On Over You"); predec.
Harambee Singers.summary: Harambee Singers is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Atlanta-based Black women's a cappella ensemble co-founded ca. 1968 by Bernice Johnson Reagon, Jamila Jones, and Matti Casey Pierce; performed Pan-African / Black Power-era repertoire ("Hands Off Nkrumah," "We'll Move On Over You"); predecessor ensemble to Sweet Honey in the Rock (founded 1973 by Reagon); distinct from SNCC Freedom Singers; documented first in this transcript as a co-founder's first-person account. Why it belongs: Canonical bridge between 1960s civil-rights-movement song tradition and 1970s Pan-Africanist / Black-feminist music organizing; Whisper-prone aliases are extraordinarily varied: "Harlem Bay Singles," "Parabai singers," "Army singers," "Harry" - four distinct Whisper renderings within a single transcript. High-damage cross-corpus risk if not cataloged. Transcript evidence: "myself, along with another singer… Matti Casey Pierce, Pierce Now, went to Bernie's house knocked on the door and said, we'd like to just continue to sing… And thus far, we started the Harambee Singers." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 58.

Hollis Watkins.description: Foundational McComb / Mississippi SNCC field worker 1961-64; pioneered Pike County voter-registration with Curtis Hayes and Bob Moses; later Mississippi Freedom Schools organizer
Hollis Watkins.summary: Hollis Watkins is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Foundational McComb / Mississippi SNCC field worker 1961-64; pioneered Pike County voter-registration with Curtis Hayes and Bob Moses; later Mississippi Freedom Schools organizer Additional Pass 7 role notes: SNCC field secretary from McComb, Mississippi; co-organizer with Curtis Hayes of the foundational SNCC McComb voter-registration project (the first SNCC voter-registration project in Mississippi); freedom song leader; lodged with the Dahmers during the early 1960s voter-registration push. Why it belongs: McLaurin's testimony (SRT implied via row 17.P2T.30-31 + "Highless" Whisper variant) provides first-person attribution of Watkins as the canonical McComb-period figure. The "Highless" garble (row 17.P2T.30) is the primary Whisper failure pattern for his name in this corpus. His role as the first SNCC organizer arrested in Mississippi (McComb, 1961) is corpus-wide significant. Not in corpus. Hollis Watkins is one of the most frequently cross-referenced figures in the SNCC Mississippi oral history subcorpus; his absence creates a recurring lookup gap. Entry 36 is a canonical primary-source confirmation of his presence at the Dahmer home. Transcript evidence: Transcript verbatim: "Hollis Watkins and Curtis Hayes (Curtis Muhammad) and Curtis Hayes (Curtis Muhammad) and Hollis Watkins out of McComb came and stayed." (The corrected transcript repeats the pattern as a Whisper loop; the underlying fact is established.) Pass 1 #36.15 marks "correct." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 17, 36, 111.

He bridges the canonical Movement generation (James Forman Sr., already in corpus) and contemporary civil-rights-successor scholarship. Transcript evidence: Row 30.P2T.1 (Pass 2 tail, high): "James Forman Jr. is a professor at Yale. Role or context: U.S. Representative (D-MI, Detroit), 1965-2017. Introduced the Martin Luther King Jr. federal holiday bill (enacted 1983), the shorter-work-week bill, and HR 676 (Expanded and Improved Medicare for All Act, first introduced 2003). Canonical Black congressional figure of the long Civil Rights era. Why it belongs: Conyers is mentioned by multiple corpus interviewees as the sponsor of HR 676, a cause that several 2013-era interviewees (including Tillow) were actively organizing around at the time of their interviews. He also introduced the MLK federal holiday - a canonical cross-corpus Civil Rights legislative landmark. His absence from the 140-entry corpus is a clear gap for the 2013 interview cohort. Transcript evidence: "HR 676 sponsored by the wonderful congressman, John Conyers, who also introduced the Martin Luther King holiday bill." / "he introduced this bill, which would remove the private for-profit insurance industry from our health care system." Multiple additional occurrences. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 74.

Joseph L. Rauh Jr..description: UAW general counsel, ADA co-founder, lead attorney in the 1964 MFDP credentials challenge at the Atlantic City DNC.

Why it belongs: Thelwell's transcript is one of the most detailed first-person accounts of the MFDP Atlantic City strategy. Rauh appears throughout the Anderson Todd transcript (variously as "Joe Rao," "Rao") and is a recurring canonical figure across multiple MFDP-related transcripts in this archive. Anderson Todd's LBJ-tapes finding that Rauh offered to withdraw as MFDP counsel before the convention (letter to the. Transcript evidence: Pass 2T rows .P2T.46, .P2T.98: "Joe Roe and Karen Smith" / "Joe Rao felt that very strong." Pass 4 verified Joseph L. Rauh Jr. (1911-92) as UAW general counsel and ADA chair. The Thelwell transcript is the richest Rauh primary-source account in the LoC corpus. Corrected transcript byte 72564: "What I found astounding was that he offered to withdraw in a letter to the White House. But he said, but I advise that I not withdraw because then it would be run by the commies." Byte 71876: "he did a brilliant job at the credentials committee hearing." Julius Wesley Becton Jr..description: Three-star U.S. Army general; first Black commander of VII Corps Germany; FEMA Director under Reagan (1985-89); Prairie View A&M University president (1989-95); DC public schools superintendent (1996-98)
Julius Wesley Becton Jr..summary: Julius Wesley Becton Jr. is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Three-star U.S. Army general; first Black commander of VII Corps Germany; FEMA Director under Reagan (1985-89); Prairie View A&M University president (1989-95); DC public schools superintendent (1996-98) Why it belongs: Becton is this entry's subject and one of the most consequential Black military-integration figures of the post-WWII era. He was the first Black VII Corps commander (one of NATO's most senior field commands), a first-person witness to the de facto integration of the 9th Infantry Regiment in Korea 1950 (two years before formal E.O. 9981 enforcement), to the Aberdeen Proving Ground post commander's defiance of E.O. 9981 in 1948, and to the Van Horn TX sheriff encounter - all canonical civil-rights-through-military-integration testimony. His FEMA directorship and post-Army public-service career place him at the intersection of military integrat. Transcript evidence: Pervasive across entire 129KB transcript; hundreds of direct references. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 71.

Junius Williams.description: Newark NJ civil rights attorney, community organizer, and legal scholar; founder of Newark Area Planning Association (NAPA); Director of Newark Model Cities Program 1970-73; National Bar Association president 1978; Rutgers-Newark professor.
Junius Williams.summary: Junius Williams is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Newark NJ civil rights attorney, community organizer, and legal scholar; founder of Newark Area Planning Association (NAPA); Director of Newark Model Cities Program 1970-73; National Bar Association president 1978; Rutgers-Newark professor; author of Unfinished Agenda (2014). Why it belongs: Williams is the subject of this transcript and a canonical figure in the Northern civil rights movement. His Mount Holyoke "Reform or Revolution?" conference (Feb 12, 1965, nine days before Malcolm X's assassination) is a documented first-person account of Northern-campus civil rights organizing. His NAPA leadership of the CMDNJ medical-school-siting fight is canonical Northern urban-renewal civil rights case. His Gibson mayoral campaign management, Kilby Prison arrest with Worth Long, and NBA presidency are all independently verifiable milestones. Transcript evidence: Direct first-person throughout; birth year sub 38; NAPA founding confirmed; Model Cities directorship confirmed; NBA presidency age-34 confirmed ("I was his first campaign manager. 1970" re: Gibson). This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 72.

Justice Arthur J. Goldberg.description: Associate Justice, U.S. Supreme Court, 1962-65 (appointed by JFK, succeeded Felix Frankfurter; succeeded by Abe Fortas when Goldberg resigned to become UN Ambassador). Prior Secretary of Labor 1961-62. Joined the unanimous New York Times v.
Justice Arthur J. Goldberg.summary: Justice Arthur J. Goldberg is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Associate Justice, U.S. Supreme Court, 1962-65 (appointed by JFK, succeeded Felix Frankfurter; succeeded by Abe Fortas when Goldberg resigned to become UN Ambassador). Prior Secretary of Labor 1961-62. Joined the unanimous New York Times v. Sullivan opinion (March 9, 1964). The Jewish Justice in Lowery's first-person testimony. Why it belongs: The corrected transcript directly surfaces Goldberg as the questioning justice in the Sullivan oral argument via Lowery's unambiguous first-person testimony ("One of the justices was Jewish" - Brennan was Catholic; Goldberg was Jewish). Pass 4 resolved the Pass 2/3 Brennan attribution to Goldberg with high confidence. Goldberg is a key figure in the mid-century First Amendment-law history that the Sullivan corpus threads. Transcript evidence: Line 627 of .srt (corrected transcript): "One of the justices was Jewish. And the little lawyer, (M.) Roland Nachman who was representing the city of Montgomery." Lonnie C. King.description: Founder and chair of the Atlanta Student Movement (Feb 1960); chair, Committee on the Appeal for Human Rights (COAHR); co-founder, Atlanta Inquirer (1960); jailed with MLK Jr. Oct 1960 in the canonical arrest triggering the JFK-Coretta cal.
Lonnie C. King.summary: Lonnie C. King is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Founder and chair of the Atlanta Student Movement (Feb 1960); chair, Committee on the Appeal for Human Rights (COAHR); co-founder, Atlanta Inquirer (1960); jailed with MLK Jr. Oct 1960 in the canonical arrest triggering the JFK-Coretta call. Why it belongs: King is the most under-foregrounded canonical founder of a major city-level sit-in movement in the corpus. His first-person account of the Feb 1, 1960 Yates and Milton Drugs moment, the commissioning of Roslyn Pope to write the Appeal, and the Warren Memorial mass meeting (March 10, 1961) are primary-source canonical testimony on events documented nowhere else at this level of granularity. As COAHR chair, he is the organizational counterpart to the Greensboro Four for the Atlanta movement - a gap in the corpus that affects hallucination-scoring accuracy for any Atlanta-centered transcript. Transcript evidence: Corrected transcript extensively documents his role in first person. The corrected transcript confirms his Morehouse enrollment, founding role, Appeal commissioning, and Oct 1960 arrest with MLK. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 80.

M. Roland Nachman Jr..description: Montgomery, Alabama attorney; lead counsel for L.B. Sullivan (Montgomery Public Safety Commissioner) in New York Times v. Sullivan, both at trial and before the U.S. Supreme Court. His closing argument to the Alabama jury - invoking Sammy.

The transcript's "Knockman" Whisper-rendering (Pass 1 #66.24) documents his name across one of the most important civil-liberties cases in U.S. history. Transcript evidence: Corrected transcript: "this lawyer named (M.) Roland Nachman who was representing the city of Montgomery… when (M.) Roland Nachman was making his summer to the jury, he said, you ministers were sitting there with an A.R. of engine innocence." NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S.description: Landmark U.S. Supreme Court decision unanimously holding that politically motivated economic boycotts are constitutionally protected First Amendment activity; arose directly from the 1966 Port Gibson, MS boycott of white merchants that thi.
NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S.summary: NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S. 886 is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Landmark U.S. Supreme Court decision unanimously holding that politically motivated economic boycotts are constitutionally protected First Amendment activity; arose directly from the 1966 Port Gibson, MS boycott of white merchants that this interview documents. Why it belongs: The Charles Evers entry (civil_rights_facts.json line 633) already mentions Claiborne Hardware as a subordinate clause in the Charles Evers summary ("The Port Gibson boycott in particular produced the foundational federal-constitutional case NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co., 458 U.S. 886 (1982)"). But the case deserves its own standalone canonical-events entry because: (a) it is the only major Supreme Court civil-rights holding in the CRHP corpus that is not yet a top-level entry; (b) Whisper renders the case name in at least six distinct ways across this transcript alone ("play born hardware case," "the end of the way," "end of the ACP," etc. Transcript evidence: "you talk to hear about the NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware Co. 458 U.S. 886 Co. Yes. So that was filed by the white merchants 69 to try to stop the boycott." Also: "they were trying to keep Evers and the NAACP out of Port Gibson." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 12.

New Communities Inc..description: Canonical 5,735-acre cooperative farm in Lee County GA, co-founded by Charles Sherrod, Shirley Sherrod, Slater King, and Bob Swann in 1969 following Israeli-kibbutz study visits; first community land trust in the U.S. South; foundational m.
New Communities Inc..summary: New Communities Inc. is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Canonical 5,735-acre cooperative farm in Lee County GA, co-founded by Charles Sherrod, Shirley Sherrod, Slater King, and Bob Swann in 1969 following Israeli-kibbutz study visits; first community land trust in the U.S. South; foundational model for the national Black-cooperative-farm movement; lost to USDA/ASCS discriminatory foreclosure in the mid-1980s; the principal plaintiff entity in the Pigford class-action. Additional Pass 7 role notes: First community land trust in the United States (1969); 6,000-acre cooperative farm, Lee County, Georgia; Kibbutz/Moshav-inspired but family-based model; founded by Charles and Shirley Sherrod, Slater King (financing), and Bob Swann (national land-trust architecture). Lost to USDA-FmHA discriminatory foreclosure 1985; received $12 million Pigford II settlem. Why it belongs: Foundational institution for the entire US community-land-trust and Black-cooperative-farm movement; first-person founding testimony in this transcript is irreplaceable Smithsonian-grade primary source. Transcript evidence: "The Southwest Georgia project, New Communities Inc., which was the land, it was a project, all of the Southwest Georgia project...the idea was to purchase this huge tract of land and become self-sufficient for Black communities." "we ended up creating an organization that we called new communities"; 6,000-acre plot confirmed; Slater King and Bob Swann roles confirmed; Kibbutz/Moshav adaptation confirmed. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 114, 117.

Pigford v. Glickman.description: Canonical 1999-2010 USDA race-discrimination class-action lawsuit; Track A ($50K capped) and Track B (full documentation) settlement structure; roughly 22,000 Track A claimants; $1.25 billion Pigford II settlement. Largest civil-rights set.
Pigford v. Glickman.summary: Pigford v. Glickman is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Canonical 1999-2010 USDA race-discrimination class-action lawsuit; Track A ($50K capped) and Track B (full documentation) settlement structure; roughly 22,000 Track A claimants; $1.25 billion Pigford II settlement. Largest civil-rights settlement in US history at the time of Pigford II. Why it belongs: Multiple transcripts in the corpus reference this litigation; it is the central federal-legal capstone of the Black-farmer civil-rights arc covered across dozens of interviews. Not currently in the 140-entry corpus despite being a canonical post-Movement federal-litigation precedent. Transcript evidence: Extended first-person account of Track A/B structure, 10-year New Communities battle, $12M victory; "six test cases," disbarred Texas attorney, monitor's office, chief adjudicator role all confirmed. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 117.

Pigford v. Glickman + Pigford II.description: The foundational Black-farmer class-action lawsuit against USDA / ASCS for systematic discriminatory loan denial; settled Pigford I (1999, ~$1.25 billion, ~16,000 claimants) and Pigford II (via December 2009 Claims Resolution Act, an addit.

The longest-running Black-farmer civil-rights litigation in U.S. history. Transcript evidence: "the government finally admitted that there was discrimination against black farmers...we came on a class action suit...the final thing came through in 20, 2009, 2009." Ralph Bunche.summary: Ralph Bunche is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: UN Under-Secretary-General; Nobel Peace Prize laureate (1950); first African American to win the Nobel Peace Prize; Howard University alumnus; foundational Black diplomat and internationalist. Named by Brown as one of the candidates passed over for the Howard presidency. Why it belongs: Bunche is cross-corpus relevant - any interviewee discussing the UN, international civil rights, or Howard University will reference him. The 140-entry corpus has no standalone Bunche entry despite his canonical stature. The Thurgood Marshall entry mentions Howard but not Bunche. Richmond 34 / Thalhimer's sit-in.description: Foundational early lunch-counter sit-in - Virginia Union University and VCU students who sat in at Thalhimer's department store in Richmond, VA on February 22, 1960, approximately three weeks after the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-ins.
Richmond 34 / Thalhimer's sit-in.summary: Richmond 34 / Thalhimer's sit-in is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Foundational early lunch-counter sit-in - Virginia Union University and VCU students who sat in at Thalhimer's department store in Richmond, VA on February 22, 1960, approximately three weeks after the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-ins. The Richmond 34 were among the first documented sit-in cohorts of the 1960 spring wave; 34 students were arrested. Why it belongs: The corpus has the Greensboro sit-ins entry but no corresponding entry for the Richmond 34, which is a distinct event with its own historiography (Virginia Union University, Hill Tucker & Martin law firm, Charles Sherrod as a co-organizer). Entry 48 (Grinnell) provides first-person testimony; Entry 18 (Sherrod) cross-corroborates. The event is documented in standard Richmond civil rights historiography and the Virginia Union University historical record. Multiple CRHP transcripts reference it. Transcript evidence: "We went downtown several days and we went to Thalhimer's department store and we sat down." / "34" (speaker's own count) / interviewer confirms "Virginia Union University students was about three weeks after the one in Greensboro." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 48.

Robert B. Hayling.description: Dentist; founder of the modern St. Augustine civil rights movement; NAACP Youth Council advisor; recruited MLK to St. Augustine; Air Force dental officer. Central figure of the entire 1963-64 St. Augustine campaign.
Robert B. Hayling.summary: Robert B. Hayling is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Dentist; founder of the modern St. Augustine civil rights movement; NAACP Youth Council advisor; recruited MLK to St. Augustine; Air Force dental officer. Central figure of the entire 1963-64 St. Augustine campaign. Additional Pass 7 role notes: Dentist; canonical leader of the St. Augustine, Florida civil rights movement 1963-65. NAACP Youth Council advisor 1963; broke from NAACP late 1963 and pivoted to SCLC; organized the May-June 1964 Dr. King visits to St. Augustine; central figure in the events that drove Civil Rights Act passage July 2, 1964. Why it belongs: This is the single highest-priority corpus addition from the entire St. Augustine sub-corpus. Hayling appears in nearly every St. Augustine entry across Passes 1-4 under multiple Whisper-mangled forms (Dr. Haillin, Dr. Helen, Dr. Harvey Haley, Dr. Arby Helen, Dr. R.B. Hayling, Dr. Cain/Ken/Martha Luther King misattributions). The scorer cannot reliably ground accuracy claims about the St. Augustine campaign without this entry. Not currently in civil_rights_facts.json (confirmed by Pass 7 corpus check). Transcript evidence: Corrected transcript: named throughout as "Dr. Robert B. Hayling"; direct-address, third-person reference, and biographical detail abundant. Whisper variants indexed in rows 4.2, 4.P2.1, 4.P4.1, 4.P4.36. Entire interview; confirmed by Pass 4 fact-check against Colburn (1985), Warren (2008), Florida Times-Union obituary. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 4, 5, 99, 103.

Robert W. Spike.description: Executive director, National Council of Churches Commission on Religion and Race; organized NCC's logistical and financial support for Freedom Summer 1964; murdered in an unsolved homicide, Columbus OH, October 17, 1966.
Robert W. Spike.summary: Robert W. Spike is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Executive director, National Council of Churches Commission on Religion and Race; organized NCC's logistical and financial support for Freedom Summer 1964; murdered in an unsolved homicide, Columbus OH, October 17, 1966. Additional Pass 7 role notes: Director, National Council of Churches Commission on Religion and Race, 1963-65. Why it belongs: Thelwell's transcript is the most detailed first-person account of Spike's role in Thelwell's corpus. Spike appears 6+ times (rendered "Bob Spake," "Robert Spake," "Bob Speck"). Pass 4 verified all biographical facts. He is absent from civil_rights_facts.json. His NCC role is structurally important to the institutional-religion dimension of Freedom Summer and the VRA campaign. Spike is the canonical white-clergy civil-rights-coordination figure of the 1963-65 period. His NCC Commission on Religion and Race was the institutional bridge between mainline Protestant denominations and SNCC/CORE/SCLC. He appears in this transcript as McNichols's d. Transcript evidence: Pass 2T row .P2T.41: "after discussions with Robert Spake and the Arabisans [rabbis]"; .P2T.111: "Bob Speck said, glad you came." Pass 6 confirmed. "I said to Steve Robbins... to the commission on religion and race, National Council of Churches, and trying to have a relationship with them to do this... he put me in touch with Art Thomas, who was head of the Delta Ministry." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 34, 118.

Roslyn Pope.description: Spelman College student; principal author of "An Appeal for Human Rights" (March 9, 1960) - the manifesto that launched the Atlanta Student Movement and was reprinted in the New York Times, The Nation, the Harvard Crimson, and entered into.
Roslyn Pope.summary: Roslyn Pope is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Spelman College student; principal author of "An Appeal for Human Rights" (March 9, 1960) - the manifesto that launched the Atlanta Student Movement and was reprinted in the New York Times, The Nation, the Harvard Crimson, and entered into the Congressional Record by Sen. Jacob Javits. Why it belongs: Pope is the sole principal author of one of the most-reprinted Movement manifestos, yet she is systematically under-foregrounded relative to the manifesto's institutional sponsors (the AU Center presidents). The corrected transcript contains first-person testimony from King about commissioning Pope and Pope's single-night authorship at Howard Zinn's home. The corpus has no entry on the Appeal itself or its author - a critical gap for a project in which the Atlanta Student Movement is a major institutional thread. Transcript evidence: Corrected transcript: "I pointed Roslyn Pope to edit it... Roslyn is so brilliant... she wrote it. And then she typed it at Dr. Zin's house, Howard Zinn." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 80.

Rudy Shields.description: Chicago-via-Vicksburg SNCC/NAACP statewide boycott organizer; Charles Evers's chief operational architect for the Mississippi boycott strategy (Natchez 1965, Port Gibson 1966, Fayette 1966); protagonist of Emilye Crosby's A Little Taste of.
Rudy Shields.summary: Rudy Shields is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Chicago-via-Vicksburg SNCC/NAACP statewide boycott organizer; Charles Evers's chief operational architect for the Mississippi boycott strategy (Natchez 1965, Port Gibson 1966, Fayette 1966); protagonist of Emilye Crosby's A Little Taste of Freedom: The Black Freedom Struggle in Claiborne County, Mississippi (UNC Press, 2005). Additional Pass 7 role notes: SW Mississippi NAACP organizer and field deputy to Charles Evers in the 1960s-70s; key figure in the Claiborne County and Port Gibson voter-registration drives. Why it belongs: Referenced directly by every Claiborne County interviewee in the CRHP corpus. The corpus's Charles Evers entry already references the Port Gibson boycott and NAACP v. Claiborne Hardware - Rudy Shields is the operational figure who made that boycott work on the ground. He is a canonical Mississippi movement figure who is currently an alias gap in the corpus (the Charles Evers entry does not surface Rudy Shields as a searchable alias). Transcript evidence: "Rudy Shields was an old organiser. We're back in Chicago and stuff... He always seemed to be somebody who was very bright, okay? Seen to be committed to the community... I really honestly hope, man, that history records the contribution that Rudy Shields made to this trouble... unsung hero." Speaker confirms Shields died without the recognition he deserved. "Rudy Shields was a brilliant, brilliant, brilliant fellow ... encouraged him to go out and try to get people to reach the vote." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 12, 92.

Sam Bowers.description: Imperial Wizard of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan of Mississippi 1964-late 1960s; ordered the January 10, 1966 firebombing of Vernon Dahmer Sr.'s Kelly Settlement home; also implicated in the June 21, 1964 Mississippi Burning murder.
Sam Bowers.summary: Sam Bowers is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Imperial Wizard of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan of Mississippi 1964-late 1960s; ordered the January 10, 1966 firebombing of Vernon Dahmer Sr.'s Kelly Settlement home; also implicated in the June 21, 1964 Mississippi Burning murders of Chaney/Goodman/Schwerner; finally convicted of ordering the Dahmer murder in August 1998 (32 years after the killing), died at Parchman 2006. Why it belongs: Currently referenced inside the Vernon Dahmer Sr. corpus entry but has no own entry. Bowers is the canonical Mississippi KKK Imperial Wizard of the 1960s - more operationally consequential than any other single Klan figure in the corpus (responsible for both the Dahmer firebombing and the Mississippi Burning murders). Entry 36 is the primary first-person victim testimony entry for the Bowers-ordered Dahmer attack; Bowers warrants a standalone corpus entry for cross-entry retrieval. Transcript evidence: Corpus Vernon Dahmer Sr. entry: "Sam Bowers, Imperial Wizard of the White Knights of the Ku Klux Klan in Mississippi, ordered the firebombing." Direct cross-reference in Entry 36 context. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 36.

Shelby County v. Holder.description: U.S. Supreme Court 5-4 decision, June 25, 2013, invalidating Section 4(b) of the Voting Rights Act of 1965 (the coverage formula). Effectively gutted the preclearance requirement for covered jurisdictions under Section 5. Tillow's intervie.
Shelby County v. Holder.summary: Shelby County v. Holder is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: U.S. Supreme Court 5-4 decision, June 25, 2013, invalidating Section 4(b) of the Voting Rights Act of 1965 (the coverage formula). Effectively gutted the preclearance requirement for covered jurisdictions under Section 5. Tillow's interview took place August 14, 2013 - less than seven weeks after the ruling - and her reference to "the voting rights decision is just heartbreaking" is direct temporal testimony. Why it belongs: Multiple 2013-cohort CRHP interviewees will reference this ruling as a fresh wound. A corpus entry for Shelby County v. Holder gives the scorer's factual-claim anchor for any summary that mentions "the voting rights decision," "the Supreme Court gutted the VRA," or similar claims in summer/fall 2013 interviews. Transcript evidence: "And certainly within the labor union movement and Shelby County v. Holder, 570 U.S. 529. And we have to get ourselves together and refight that battle." (The corrected transcript marks the canonical case name inline.) This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 74.

Shirley Miller Sherrod.description: SNCC organizer (Baker County, 1965-1966); co-founder and director of New Communities Inc. cooperative farm (1969-1985); Georgia state director, Federation of Southern Cooperatives (1985-2009); USDA Rural Development State Director for Geor.
Shirley Miller Sherrod.summary: Shirley Miller Sherrod is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: SNCC organizer (Baker County, 1965-1966); co-founder and director of New Communities Inc. cooperative farm (1969-1985); Georgia state director, Federation of Southern Cooperatives (1985-2009); USDA Rural Development State Director for Georgia (2009-2010); first-person witness to her father Hosea Miller's March 1965 murder by Cal Hall. Why it belongs: Canonical 21st-century civil-rights and agricultural-justice figure; her 2010 USDA firing-and-reinstatement controversy (Breitbart deceptive edit) is one of the most-documented post-Movement civil-rights-media episodes; her New Communities Inc. founding is the origin testimony for the US community-land-trust and Black-cooperative-farm movement; she is the subject of this interview and among the most significant figures in the corpus. Transcript evidence: Throughout - name confirmed, birth year (1948 implied), all canonical events confirmed by first-person account. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 117.

Simeon Booker.description: First Black reporter at the Washington Post (1952); Jet magazine Washington DC bureau chief from mid-1950s through the 1980s; firsthand journalist-witness on the Emmett Till trial (1955), Little Rock crisis (1957), and 1961 Freedom Rides (.
Simeon Booker.summary: Simeon Booker is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Booker is a foundational Black-press figure who documented Movement history from the inside. He was also an honorary pall-bearer at JFK's funeral (November 25, 1963). The pipeline's fact-checker will need an alias list to recognize Whisper renderings of his name. Transcript evidence: Full transcript - Booker is a co-subject and speaks throughout. Seay Sr said that night to me… And he said, we're going to do like the doodle bird." Also: "I was with Reving Sey, Sey, who was… had the first meeting for the Montgomery Bus Boycott." Role or context: MLK's white financial adviser, ghostwriter, and closest non-movement confidant; central pretext for FBI/COINTELPRO surveillance of King; subject of JFK's June 1963 warning to MLK; stepped back from direct contact with King under pressure to protect the movement Why it belongs: Levison appears repeatedly in the Dream-speech and March-on-Washington passages of the transcript. The 1919 Elaine Massacre.description: Mass racial violence event in Elaine (Phillips County), AR; estimated 100-800 Black people killed by white mobs and federal troops in October 1919; precipitated by the murder of Black sharecroppers' union organizers at Hoop Spur; suppresse.
The 1919 Elaine Massacre.summary: The 1919 Elaine Massacre is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Mass racial violence event in Elaine (Phillips County), AR; estimated 100-800 Black people killed by white mobs and federal troops in October 1919; precipitated by the murder of Black sharecroppers' union organizers at Hoop Spur; suppressed in Arkansas collective memory for decades. Why it belongs: The Elaine Massacre is the defining historical trauma of Phillips County, where this interview is set (Barton, Lexa, West Helena, Helena - all Phillips County). Speaker references it at pos 20952-21719 and the corrected transcript confirms Mosnier's framing: "That was obviously the location of the Elaine Massacre. 1919." It grounds the AR movement context the way the 1955 Till murder grounds the MS movement context. The Whisper rendering "Elaine Masker" (Pass 1 row 13.7) recurs in other AR-Phillips-County entries. Not in the 140-entry corpus despite being directly relevant to the CRHP's AR interview cohort. Transcript evidence: Pos 20952 (Mosnier introduces it), pos 21084 (speaker recalls first learning about it in her middle teens while working in the area; describes "blood in the earth"), pos 38150 (Snow Lake, near Elaine, mentioned as the site of her cotton-field assault). This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 13.

The Bennett Belles.description: The group of Bennett College women who planned and conducted the February 1, 1960 Greensboro lunch counter sit-ins in parallel with - and in the parallel-planning thesis, prior to and in coordination with - the more-famous A&T Four. Named.
The Bennett Belles.summary: The Bennett Belles is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: The group of Bennett College women who planned and conducted the February 1, 1960 Greensboro lunch counter sit-ins in parallel with - and in the parallel-planning thesis, prior to and in coordination with - the more-famous A&T Four. Named members: Esther Terry (née Alexander), Roslyn Smith, Gwendolyn Mackel, Shirley Dismuke, Dolores Finger, Gloria Brown, Linda Brown. Dr. Hobart Jarrett and Dr. Willa B. Player were t. Why it belongs: The existing corpus entry for "Greensboro sit-ins" (civil_rights_facts.json line 101-104) attributes the sit-ins exclusively to the A&T Four and does not mention the Bennett College women. Terry's transcript is the primary first-person source for the parallel-planning thesis, which is a significant historiographical correction to the spontaneous-A&T narrative. The Smithsonian/LoC publication context makes this omission particularly consequential. Transcript evidence: "students were planning and talking about a boycott... it had come, that action had been born of a plan, that had been carefully, carefully considered and deliberated beginning here on Bennett's campus with the girls... Gwen Mackel... said I was in Woolworths... because we were there... it was a plan." Extensive - this is the central subject of the interview. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 40.

Theodore \"Hugo\" Brooks.description: Houston Black businessman, civil-rights bail-bondsman, and $35,000 land-bond financier of the Sept 1961 Houston Freedom Ride.
Theodore \"Hugo\" Brooks.summary: Theodore "Hugo" Brooks is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Houston Black businessman, civil-rights bail-bondsman, and $35,000 land-bond financier of the Sept 1961 Houston Freedom Ride. Why it belongs: Brooks is the foundational Houston-Movement infrastructure figure. He provided the financial backbone for the Sept 1961 Freedom Ride prosecution defense. His daughter was also a Freedom Rider. No corpus entry exists for Houston Movement financing infrastructure, and Brooks is McNichols's most-cited local figure. He occupies the same structural role in the Houston Freedom Ride story that Amzie Moore occupies in the Mississippi Delta story - the local Black establishment figure who took personal financial risk to enable the national movement. Transcript evidence: "Theodore 'Hugo' Brooks. He was a great guy... He put up the necessary cash... He put up a $35,000 land bond, you know, land bond... his daughter also was a freedom writer." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 118.

Timothy Jenkins.description: Howard University NAG member / USNSA National Affairs Vice President 1960-61 / SNCC Executive Committee member / 1961 Capahosic VEP-Kennedy negotiation co-architect / 1961 Nashville first-Freedom-School organizer at Fisk / Yale Law JD / Na.
Timothy Jenkins.summary: Timothy Jenkins is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Howard University NAG member / USNSA National Affairs Vice President 1960-61 / SNCC Executive Committee member / 1961 Capahosic VEP-Kennedy negotiation co-architect / 1961 Nashville first-Freedom-School organizer at Fisk / Yale Law JD / National Conference of Black Lawyers co-founder / 1965 VRA 3-judge-panel amendment lobbyist / President of University of the District of Columbia Why it belongs: Jenkins is one of the most substantively important SNCC strategic-intellectual figures, with canonical first-person testimony on the Capahosic VEP negotiation, the 3-judge-panel VRA amendment, the "Stop in the name of Medgar" rooftop intervention, and the August 1961 Fisk Freedom School. He is not a peripheral figure; he co-shaped post-Freedom-Ride SNCC strategy at the highest internal level. His absence from the corpus creates a gap when other entries (Courtland Cox #24, Charles Sherrod #18, Chuck McDew #16, Charles Jones #101) reference the Capahosic delegation without a Jenkins anchor. Transcript evidence: Entire interview is first-person on all listed roles. Corrected transcript confirmed at key junctures by Pass 4 spot-checks. U.S. v. Lynd.description: The foundational Justice Department pre-Voting-Rights-Act voting-rights case against Forrest County (Hattiesburg) Circuit Clerk Theron Lynd; established the 18-plaintiff voter-registration relief order; one of the legal precedents the 1965.
U.S. v. Lynd.summary: U.S. v. Lynd is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: The foundational Justice Department pre-Voting-Rights-Act voting-rights case against Forrest County (Hattiesburg) Circuit Clerk Theron Lynd; established the 18-plaintiff voter-registration relief order; one of the legal precedents the 1965 VRA's enforcement provisions were modeled on. Prosecuted by John Doar under RFK and Katzenbach. Why it belongs: Currently referenced only inside the Theron Lynd and John Doar corpus entries. The case itself warrants a standalone entry alongside Brown v. Board, Plessy v. Ferguson, Loving v. Virginia, and other case entries already in corpus. Entry 36 is the only first-person plaintiff testimony in the corpus for this case. Transcript evidence: Transcript: "I was one of the 18 that he had to let register. That he had to let issued the voter registration card to we didn't have to go back and register in the mail." Plus the entire Lynd cross-examination sequence, the Fifth Circuit appeal, and the posthumous voter-card detail. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 36.

Virginia Foster Durr.description: White civil rights ally in Montgomery AL; wife of Clifford Durr (former FCC commissioner); sister-in-law of US Supreme Court Justice Hugo Black; personally bailed Rosa Parks out of Montgomery City Jail on December 1, 1955; foundational ant.
Virginia Foster Durr.summary: Virginia Foster Durr is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. She appears in Whisper as "Virginia Dure" - a trivial but certain Whisper failure on the surname "Durr" that will recur in any interview mentioning her. She is not in the 140-entry corpus despite being a canonical-figure level figure in the Montgomery and white-Southern-ally tradition. Transcript evidence: Corrected transcript: "Virginia Durr but miss Baker, you know these kind of people that had principles, you know that weren't Compromises." Pass 4 row 124.P4.9 confirms the catch. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Walter Reuther.summary: Walter Reuther is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: President, United Auto Workers (UAW), 1946-1970; central figure in the labor-civil-rights coalition; canonical participant in the Aug 25-26, 1964 Atlantic City MFDP pressure operation. Why it belongs: Reuther appears by name (variously garbled as "Ruther," "Ruther and Ruther free") at multiple points in the Anderson Todd transcript. Anderson Todd's distinctive scholarly claim - based on 7+ years of LBJ-tapes research - is that Reuther, not Humphrey, was the principal architect of the "compromise" imposed on the MFDP delegation at Atlantic City. Getting the name right is load-bearing for the Smithsonian summary and for any downstream scorer evaluating the accuracy of claims about the 1964 Atlantic City convention. Cross-corpus relevance: Reuther is likely referenced (often garbled) in multiple other MFDP-related transcripts in this archive. Transcript evidence: Corrected transcript bytes 73951 ("Walter Reuther was never enthusiastic about the challenge"), 77914 ("It was Ruther's manipulation and plan on Tuesday that made what Johnson wanted work"), 78642 ("It was take it or leave it"), 79507 ("by by had russin was conducting the meeting" - garbled Bayard Rustin/Reuther reference). Walter Rodney.summary: Walter Rodney is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Guyanese historian, revolutionary, and pan-Africanist (1942-1980); author of How Europe Underdeveloped Africa (1972); professor at University of Dar es Salaam; banned from Jamaica by the JLP in 1968 (triggering the Walter Rodney Riots in Kingston); assassinated in Georgetown, Guyana, June 13, 1980. Why it belongs: Rodney is a high-frequency reference across the corpus as a canonical figure of the Black Power / Pan-African academic generation. His 1971 IBW Atlanta lectureship (confirmed first-person by Strickland in this transcript) and his How Europe Underdeveloped Africa text are cited across multiple corpus transcripts. His assassination is a canonical 1980 political event. The corpus currently has no Rodney entry despite his recurring presence. Transcript evidence: "Walter came to some research symposium that we conducted at Atlanta. And we became great friends." + "he came up, yeah, 71 or 72" + University of Dar es Salaam + JLP ban from Jamaica confirmed in corrected transcript. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 127.

Walter White.description: NAACP Executive Secretary 1929-1955; blond-haired blue-eyed Black NAACP executive who conducted undercover lynching investigations throughout the 1930s; his 1949 interracial marriage to Poppy Cannon triggered serious organizational upheaval
Walter White.summary: Walter White is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Transcript evidence: "Walter White divorces Black wife and married a white woman that practically broke up the whole organization"; "I got to meet the secretary of the NAACP, the executive secretary Walter White and Roy Wilkins"; "Oh, Walter White. It was a wonderful administrator. But sometimes he was above all of us. It was Roy Wilkins that did the work there." Wheeler Parker Jr..description: Closest surviving eyewitness to the Aug 28, 1955 Emmett Till abduction; Emmett Till's first cousin; civil-rights advocate; minister
Wheeler Parker Jr..summary: Wheeler Parker Jr. is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Closest surviving eyewitness to the Aug 28, 1955 Emmett Till abduction; Emmett Till's first cousin; civil-rights advocate; minister Why it belongs: The corpus already has "The Lynching of Emmett Till" as an event entry but lacks the primary eyewitness. Wheeler Parker Jr. (died July 19, 2024) gave the authoritative first-person account of the Aug 24, 1955 Bryant's Grocery wolf-whistle incident and the 2:30 AM Aug 28, 1955 abduction. His account is the primary source for the definitive correction of "Emmett grabbed Carolyn Bryant inside the store" - Parker's testimony: he and Emmett exited the store before the whistle occurred on the porch. He was a 2008 Till Bill advocate and 2011 Mississippi Freedom Trail inaugural speaker. His death in 2024 makes this 2011 interview the most important. Transcript evidence: "I decided to go into the store... Emmett came in... Then the both of them came out of the store. And after they came out... Carolyn Bryant Donham came out... And Emmett whistled." + "I guess about two, two-thirty and more in my grandfather's house... they came in and this guy had his pistol and one hand and a flashlight... And they walk past me." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 125.

Z. Alexander Looby.summary: Z. Alexander Looby is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Nashville, Tennessee civil rights attorney; lead attorney for desegregation of Tennessee public schools; Meharry Medical College jurisprudence instructor. His home was dynamited April 19, 1960, triggering the canonical 4,000-person silent march on Nashville City Hall led by Diane Nash, John Lewis, C.T. Vivian, and James Bevel - a foundational Nashville Movement event. Transcript evidence: "my jurisprudence or dental law instructor was Z. Alexander Looby…he was the lead attorney for the desegregation of the Nashville of the Tennessee area public schools…having his home dynamite it…the morning that they dynamited the front part of his house blew out windows of the dormitory." This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 103.

Z.T. Mathews (Sheriff Zebulon Thomas Mat.description: Terrell County GA sheriff; canonical antagonist of the SW Georgia Movement; led the armed invasion of Mt. Olive Baptist Church mass meeting, summer 1962
Z.T. Mathews (Sheriff Zebulon Thomas Mat.summary: Z.T. Mathews (Sheriff Zebulon Thomas Mathews) is included in the Civil Rights History Project ground-truth corpus from the Pass 7 Publication Readiness Review. Role or context: Terrell County GA sheriff; canonical antagonist of the SW Georgia Movement; led the armed invasion of Mt. Olive Baptist Church mass meeting, summer 1962 Why it belongs: Transcript: "ZT Matthews is what we call him. He was the sheriff's name." Speaker saw him in person at the Mt. Olive invasion. His "we want our colored people to live like they've been living for the last hundred years" quote is one of the most-cited segregationist statements. This entry exists to ground ASR correction, dual scoring, citation audit, and entity matching for the named interview passages. Pass 7 proposal source entries: 23.
